description:
## What
In lavra-work.md Phase M8, after each wave completes and tests pass, dispatch `goal-verifier` per closed bead against its Validation section. Currently goal-verifier only runs in the single-bead path.

Interpret results:
- CRITICAL failures (Exists/Substantive) → reopen the bead, return to implementation
- WARNING (Wired/anti-patterns) → note in PR description, proceed

Orchestrator already has bead details in memory at this point — no extra fetch needed.

## Why
Drift between spec and implementation accumulates silently across waves. By the time lavra-review runs post-epic, there are 15 fix commits worth of rework. Catching per-bead at wave-close is cheaper than batch post-review.

## Files
- `plugins/lavra/commands/lavra-work.md` — Phase M8 only
title: Multi-bead: add per-bead goal-verifier dispatch in Phase M8

description:
Implement subagent wrapup in the OpenCode plugin using tool.execute.after filtered to tool="task".

## Research Findings (confirmed via DeepWiki + OpenCode source)

The original assumption that session.idle was the only option was WRONG. OpenCode has a clean path:

### How it works
- tool.execute.after fires with tool="task" when a TaskTool (subagent) completes
- The payload includes:
  - title: task description
  - output: final text output from subagent (may include <task_metadata> block with session ID)
  - metadata.summary: summary of tool calls within the subagent session
  - metadata.sessionID: the subagent session ID
  - metadata.model: model used by subagent
  - attachments: optional FilePart array

### Implementation approach
In plugin.js, add a filter in the tool.execute.after handler:

```javascript
if (input.tool === "task") {
  // This is a subagent completion
  // 1. Check output/metadata.summary for BEAD_ID pattern
  // 2. Check if subagent logged any knowledge comments (bd comments add)
  // 3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge logged, inject reminder
  //    (cannot block like Claude Code SubagentStop, but can log warning)
}
```

### Key differences from Claude Code SubagentStop
1. Cannot "block" completion - tool.execute.after is post-execution, not a gate
2. CAN inspect the full subagent output and tool call summary
3. CAN inject a follow-up system message reminding to log knowledge
4. CAN log a warning comment on the bead automatically

### Behavioral design
Since we cannot block (like subagent-wrapup.sh does with decision: block), the OpenCode version should:
1. Parse output + metadata.summary for BEAD_ID references
2. Check if any bd comments add calls appear in the summary
3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge captured:
   - Auto-add a reminder comment to the bead: "WARNING: Subagent completed without logging knowledge"
   - Return output with appended reminder text via the hook output
4. If knowledge WAS captured, no action needed

### Integration with plugin.js
This is an additional filter branch in the existing tool.execute.after handler (already needed for memory-capture). Estimated: ~20 lines added to plugin.js.

## Files to modify
- plugins/beads-compound/opencode/plugin.js (add task tool filter branch)
title: Implement subagent wrapup via tool.execute.after (tool=task)

description:
Inspired by Mario Zechner's observations on agent-driven coding: the bottleneck is review quality, not code generation speed. Agents drift when specs are vague, not when specs are long.

Changes:
1. Remove 150-line description cap from /lavra-plan and /lavra-design. Replace with a completeness gate that validates bead descriptions answer: What exactly changes? What decisions are already made? What does done look like? What should the agent NOT do?
2. Add ~1000 LOC change cap per bead as a scope gate on output (not input). If a bead would touch >1000 lines, recommend splitting.
3. Add --no-chain flag to /lavra-work for opt-in review pauses between beads in multi-bead mode.
4. Description quality gate at plan time: missing required sections = warning, not just advisory.
title: Replace size gates with quality gates for bead descriptions

description:
Add cross-platform compatibility to the beads-compound plugin, starting with OpenCode and Gemini CLI.

## Platforms

### OpenCode (TypeScript plugin)
- Plugin system: TypeScript files in .opencode/plugins/ (Bun-native, no build step)
- Event mapping: session.created, tool.execute.after, shell.env
- Subagent wrapup: tool.execute.after with tool="task" filter (confirmed via DeepWiki)
- Reads: AGENTS.md
- Install: manual copy of plugin.ts + package.json

### Gemini CLI (settings.json hooks)
- Hook system: Nearly identical to Claude Code (JSON stdin/stdout wire protocol)
- Event mapping: SessionStart, AfterTool, AfterAgent
- Has built-in Claude Code migration path in source
- Reads: GEMINI.md (configurable via context.fileName)
- Install: `gemini extensions install <github-url>` (needs gemini-extension.json manifest)

### Not yet viable
- Codex CLI: Hook system not shipped (PR #11067 closed). Similar wire protocol to Claude Code when it lands.
- Antigravity: No hook/lifecycle event system. Skills-only extension model.

## Shared foundation
- Shell scripts (auto-recall.sh, memory-capture.sh, subagent-wrapup.sh) are already portable
- Knowledge system (.beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl) is tool-agnostic
- bd CLI works in any terminal

## Key decisions
- Use Bun.spawn() not $ shell template in plugin.ts (security)
- Pre-fork filter bd comments? add regex in TypeScript before spawning subprocess (performance)
- Pin @opencode-ai/plugin to exact version (supply chain)
- AGENTS.md is open standard adopted by 20+ tools; symlink CLAUDE.md -> AGENTS.md recommended
title: Epic: Multi-platform support (OpenCode, Gemini CLI)

description:
## What

The /beads-brainstorm command currently only accepts feature descriptions as arguments. It should also support bead IDs to allow users to brainstorm on existing beads (especially vague epics that need more exploration before planning child beads).

## Expected Behavior

The command should:
1. Detect if the argument is a bead ID (matches pattern like 'beads-*' or alphanumeric segments with hyphens)
2. If bead ID detected, load bead details with `bd show {ID} --json` and extract the description
3. Use the bead's title and description as context for brainstorming
4. If not a bead ID, proceed with current behavior (treat as feature description)

## Context

- Similar to the fix applied to /beads-plan (bdcompound-nab)
- Use case: User has vague epic and wants to explore it more deeply before creating child beads
- Pattern: ^[a-z0-9]+-[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$

## Testing

- [ ] Test with epic bead ID: `/beads-brainstorm {EPIC_ID}`
- [ ] Test with feature description: `/beads-brainstorm "Add user notifications"`
- [ ] Verify bead content is loaded when ID is passed
- [ ] Verify feature description is used when non-ID text is passed
- [ ] Handle invalid bead IDs gracefully

## Validation

- [ ] Command correctly identifies bead IDs vs feature descriptions
- [ ] Bead content is extracted and used for brainstorming when ID is passed
- [ ] Original feature description behavior still works
- [ ] Error handling for invalid/non-existent bead IDs
title: Support bead ID argument in /beads-brainstorm command

description:
## Vision
Lavra owns its data in .lavra/ — a dedicated directory with its own .gitignore and .gitattributes — rather than writing into the beads-owned .beads/ directory. This enables clean separation between the two tools and makes Lavra installable on projects that don't use beads at all.

## Requirements
1. .lavra/ directory with .gitignore and .gitattributes at folder level (not subdirectory level)
2. .lavra/memory/ for knowledge.jsonl, recall.sh, knowledge-db.sh
3. .lavra/config/ for lavra.json, project-setup.md, codebase-profile.md
4. .lavra/retros/ for retro JSON snapshots
5. All hooks hard-recoded from .beads/ to .lavra/ paths (no fallback/dual-path)
6. Installer auto-migrates existing .beads/{memory,config,retros} data to .lavra/ on detection
7. Migration is copy + warn (originals left in place, user deletes after verification)
8. All commands, scripts, docs, CI updated to reference .lavra/ paths

## Non-Requirements
- No dual-path fallback logic in hooks
- No environment variable for LAVRA_DIR
- No interactive migration prompt (auto-detect + copy is silent with a warning)
- Does NOT touch .beads/ issue/comment/backup data (owned by beads CLI)

## Locked Decisions
- Folder-level .lavra/.gitignore and .lavra/.gitattributes (paths relative to .lavra/ root)
- Hard recode .beads/ → .lavra/ everywhere (no fallback detection in hooks)
- Auto-migrate on install: copy .beads/memory → .lavra/memory, .beads/config → .lavra/config, .beads/retros → .lavra/retros; leave originals with printed warning
- .lavra/ is committed to git (knowledge.jsonl, config, retros committed; SQLite cache and session-state.md gitignored)
- Implementation: one epic, sequential phase beads, each with tests and verification

## Agent Discretion
- Exact wording of the migration warning message
- Whether .lavra-version file lives at .lavra/ root or .lavra/memory/
- Order of operations within each phase bead

## Deferred
- OpenCode plugin.ts internal path changes (if the TS plugin has hardcoded .beads/ refs beyond what hooks handle)
- Cortex Code installer path updates (low priority, follow-on)

## Phases
- bdcompound-63c.2: Phase 1 — .lavra/ skeleton and provision-memory update
- bdcompound-63c.1: Phase 2 — Recode hooks from .beads/ to .lavra/
- bdcompound-63c.3: Phase 3 — Installer migration
- bdcompound-63c.4: Phase 4 — Update commands and scripts
- bdcompound-63c.5: Phase 5 — Update docs, CI, CLAUDE.md rules, release checklist
- bdcompound-63c.6: Phase 6 — Smoke tests and version bump
title: Move Lavra data directory from .beads/ to .lavra/

description:
## What
Recode all hook scripts to use .lavra/ paths instead of .beads/ paths. Three files change (auto-recall.sh, memory-capture.sh, check-memory.sh + recall.sh template). Two files need zero changes (subagent-wrapup.sh, knowledge-db.sh). Also fix three security issues and two silent-failure guard bugs in auto-recall.sh and check-memory.sh.

## Context
Hook scripts live in plugins/lavra/hooks/. After Phase 1 updates provision-memory.sh, these hooks still point to .beads/ paths and will fail. This phase updates the runtime hooks so the installed system works end-to-end.

Files that need changes:
- auto-recall.sh (SessionStart hook)
- memory-capture.sh (PostToolUse hook)
- check-memory.sh (optional check script)
- recall.sh (template search script, installed to .lavra/memory/)

Files that need ZERO changes:
- subagent-wrapup.sh — no .beads/ references (confirmed)
- knowledge-db.sh — all paths are passed as parameters, fully agnostic (confirmed)

## Specific Changes

### auto-recall.sh
- Lines 32-38: Guard 'if [ ! -d "$PROJECT_DIR/.beads" ]' — MUST change to check .lavra/ for non-beads projects. After migration, projects with .lavra/ but no .beads/ would always exit with the 'beads not initialized' hint, which is wrong — beads is no longer required. Change to: exit only if both .beads/ AND .lavra/ are absent. Update hint text to not require 'bd init' for non-beads users.
- Line 33: "$PROJECT_DIR/.beads" → "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra" in existence check
- Line 41: MEMORY_DIR path → .lavra/memory
- Line 64: .beads/memory path → .lavra/memory (version file directory lookup)
- Line 65: version file path — .lavra-version moved to .lavra/ root (not .lavra/memory/). MUST use "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/.lavra-version" NOT "$MEMORY_DIR/.lavra-version". This is the critical distinction.
- Line 199 (source annotation): update 'source=".beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl"' → '.lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl'
- Session state path: auto-resolves since it uses $MEMORY_DIR — no explicit change needed

**Security fix — H1: Replace bare heredoc JSON with jq assembly**
All inline `cat <<EOF ... $VAR ... EOF` JSON constructions in auto-recall.sh inject shell variables into JSON without full JSON escaping. A knowledge entry containing '"' followed by '}}}' can break out of the JSON string and inject arbitrary hook output fields. A newline in content produces invalid JSON that silently drops recall output.
Replace ALL bare heredoc JSON constructions with jq:
```bash
jq -cn --arg msg "$OUTPUT_MSG" '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"systemMessage":$msg}}'
```
This applies to the main output block AND every early-exit block that emits inline JSON (lines ~35, ~46, ~57, ~76, ~88).

**Security fix — M1: Apply sanitization to session-state.md**
session-state.md is AI-generated content (written by /lavra-work, /lavra-checkpoint) but is currently injected into next session's system message without the sanitization applied to $RELEVANT_KNOWLEDGE. A compromised subagent could write 'SYSTEM: <injected instruction>' to session-state.md.
Apply the same sanitization pipeline to $SESSION_STATE as to $RELEVANT_KNOWLEDGE: role-prefix stripping, bidirectional-character removal, and wrap in <untrusted-knowledge> tags.

### memory-capture.sh
- Line 89: MEMORY_DIR path → .lavra/memory
- All other references (KNOWLEDGE_FILE, knowledge.db paths) derive from MEMORY_DIR — auto-update once line 89 is changed
- Add CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR validation AFTER the existing early-exit guards (lines 18-19). CRITICAL placement: if placed BEFORE early-exit guards it runs on every single Bash call (PostToolUse fires very frequently). Placed after early-exit guards it only runs on ~1% of calls that match 'bd comment add'.

**Security fix — H3: Tighter CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR validation**
The proposed OR condition (allow if .lavra exists OR .beads exists) can be bypassed: an attacker who controls CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR only needs a directory with .beads/ to redirect knowledge writes. Use a tighter check:
```bash
if [[ -n "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" ]]; then
  CANONICAL=$(realpath -m "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" 2>/dev/null || echo "")
  if [[ -z "$CANONICAL" ]] || [[ "$CANONICAL" != /* ]] ||      [[ "$CANONICAL" != "$HOME"/* && "$CANONICAL" != /tmp/* ]]; then
    CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR=""  # fall through to CWD
  fi
fi
```
This validates the canonical path starts with a known trusted prefix.

### check-memory.sh
- Line 37: update .beads/memory/ → .lavra/memory/
- Line 38: Gate 'if [ ! -d ".beads" ]' — MUST change this primary guard. On a non-beads Lavra project (no .beads/ dir), this exits immediately and check-memory.sh never runs. Change to: 'if [ ! -d ".beads" ] && [ ! -d ".lavra" ]; then exit 0'. This is the guard, not just a path update.
- Line 97: MEMORY_DIR=".beads/memory" fallback hardcode → MEMORY_DIR=".lavra/memory" — THIS FALLBACK must be caught. It's in the else branch for missing provision script.
  NOTE: Do NOT add CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR validation here — this is the broken-install fallback path. Just change the string.
  NOTE: check-memory.sh has a separate gate at line 38 for .beads existence — that guard is now architecturally incorrect (Lavra's memory doesn't need beads). Document this as a known limitation in a comment if the full fix is deferred, but the guard MUST be updated.

### recall.sh (template, gets installed to .lavra/memory/recall.sh)
- Lines 3, 14: update .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl → .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl
  Note: recall.sh is installed BY VALUE (copied by provision-memory.sh) into the project at .lavra/memory/recall.sh. The template must be updated; re-running provision on an existing install will overwrite old copy with new one.

## Decisions

### Locked
- Hard recode — no dual-path fallback, no env var
- subagent-wrapup.sh and knowledge-db.sh are NOT modified
- .lavra-version file is at .lavra/ root (not .lavra/memory/) — auto-recall.sh must use PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/.lavra-version
- check-memory.sh line 38 guard MUST be updated (not just lines 37 and 97)
- CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR validation placement: AFTER early-exit guards in memory-capture.sh

### Discretion

description:
## What
Update provision-memory.sh to create and manage a .lavra/ directory structure instead of writing into .beads/. Create a folder-level .lavra/.gitignore and .lavra/.gitattributes (NOT subdirectory-level). This is the foundation — all other phases depend on this structure being correct.

## Context
provision-memory.sh is at plugins/lavra/hooks/provision-memory.sh. It currently:
- Creates .beads/memory/ with knowledge.jsonl, recall.sh, knowledge-db.sh, .gitattributes, .gitignore, .lavra-version
- Creates .beads/config/ with lavra.json
- Runs git add on all .beads/memory/ files
- Validates project .gitignore for .beads/ patterns (warns if present)

The new structure moves ALL of this to .lavra/, with a single .gitignore and .gitattributes at .lavra/ root (not .lavra/memory/).

## New .lavra/ Directory Structure
```
.lavra/
├── .gitignore        (NEW: folder-level, replaces .beads/memory/.gitignore)
├── .gitattributes    (NEW: folder-level, replaces .beads/memory/.gitattributes)
├── .lavra-version    (MOVED: was .beads/memory/.lavra-version)
├── memory/
│   ├── knowledge.jsonl
│   ├── knowledge.archive.jsonl
│   ├── knowledge.db           (gitignored via .lavra/.gitignore)
│   ├── knowledge.db-journal   (gitignored)
│   ├── knowledge.db-wal       (gitignored)
│   ├── knowledge.db-shm       (gitignored)
│   ├── session-state.md       (gitignored)
│   ├── recall.sh
│   └── knowledge-db.sh
├── config/
│   ├── lavra.json
│   ├── project-setup.md       (committed when created)
│   └── codebase-profile.md    (committed when created)
└── retros/
    └── {date}.json            (committed)
```

## .lavra/.gitignore content (new, folder-level)
```
# SQLite FTS cache (rebuilt from knowledge.jsonl on first use)
memory/knowledge.db
memory/knowledge.db-journal
memory/knowledge.db-wal
memory/knowledge.db-shm

# Ephemeral session state (survives compaction, recalled once, then deleted)
memory/session-state.md
```
Note: paths are relative to .lavra/ root, so they use memory/ prefix.

## .lavra/.gitattributes content (new, folder-level)
```
memory/knowledge.jsonl merge=union
memory/knowledge.archive.jsonl merge=union
```
Note: CRITICAL — paths MUST include the memory/ prefix. bare 'knowledge.jsonl' would NOT match .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl. The gitattributes must say 'memory/knowledge.jsonl merge=union'.

## Decisions

### Locked
- Folder-level .gitignore and .gitattributes at .lavra/ root (NOT .lavra/memory/)
- .lavra-version marker lives at .lavra/.lavra-version (NOT .lavra/memory/)
- Hard path to .lavra/ — no .beads/ fallback
- .lavra/ contents committed to git; SQLite cache and session-state.md gitignored
- mkdir for .lavra/retros/ must also be created

### Discretion
- Exact wording of the printed output messages
- Whether to create knowledge.archive.jsonl as empty file or let it be created on first rotation

## Specific Changes to provision-memory.sh
1. Line 18: MEMORY_DIR path → .lavra/memory
2. Line 72: CONFIG_DIR path → .lavra/config
3. Do NOT add a LAVRA_DIR variable — use "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra" inline at the two call sites. No new abstraction needed.
4. Add: mkdir -p "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/retros" in provisioning block
5. .gitattributes: write to "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/.gitattributes" (NOT MEMORY_DIR) with 'memory/knowledge.jsonl merge=union'
6. .gitignore: write to "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/.gitignore" (NOT MEMORY_DIR) with 'memory/knowledge.db' etc. (memory/ prefix paths)
7. .lavra-version: write to "$PROJECT_DIR/.lavra/.lavra-version" (NOT MEMORY_DIR)
8. Lines 97-107: Update gitignore validation — change regex from \.beads/ to \.lavra/. Update warning message text to say 'Lavra knowledge and config' (not 'beads issues, comments, and knowledge').
9. Lines 135-143: Update git add block — MUST stage .lavra/ paths:
   - .lavra/.gitattributes
   - .lavra/.gitignore
   - .lavra/.lavra-version
   - .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl
   - .lavra/memory/recall.sh
   - .lavra/memory/knowledge-db.sh
   REMOVE -f flag: use plain 'git add' (no -f) to respect user's .gitignore. The -f override can silently commit AI-generated knowledge (API keys, internal architecture notes) if user has .lavra/ gitignored in a private project.
10. Directory creation: use mkdir -p -m 700 for .lavra/memory/ to prevent world-readable permissions on knowledge.jsonl (may contain sensitive technical information). Apply same to config/ and retros/.
11. Update all echo messages to reference .lavra/ not .beads/

## Testing
- [ ] Run provision-memory.sh on fresh project — verify .lavra/ structure created correctly
- [ ] Verify .gitattributes at .lavra/ root (not .lavra/memory/), containing 'memory/knowledge.jsonl merge=union'
- [ ] Verify .gitignore at .lavra/ root, containing 'memory/knowledge.db' (with memory/ prefix)
- [ ] Verify .lavra-version at .lavra/ root (not .lavra/memory/)
- [ ] Run provision-memory.sh twice — should be idempotent (no duplicate files/dirs)
- [ ] Test gitignore validation: project with .lavra/ in .gitignore — confirm warning fires (not .beads/)
- [ ] Run 'git check-attr merge .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl' after install — must return 'merge: union'
- [ ] Verify git add stages .lavra/ files (git status shows .lavra/... tracked)
- [ ] Verify git add does NOT use -f flag (intentionally ignored .lavra/ should not be force-staged)

## Validation
- [ ] .lavra/.gitattributes content uses memory/ prefix: 'memory/knowledge.jsonl merge=union'
- [ ] .lavra/.gitignore content uses memory/ prefix: 'memory/knowledge.db'
- [ ] .lavra-version is at .lavra/ root, not .lavra/memory/
- [ ] git add block stages .lavra/ paths without -f flag
- [ ] No -f flag on git add (security: respect user's gitignore)
- [ ] mkdir uses -m 700 for .lavra/memory/, .lavra/config/, .lavra/retros/
- [ ] Version string '0.7.0' still matches LAVRA_VERSION in auto-recall.sh (just path changed)

## Files
- plugins/lavra/hooks/provision-memory.sh

## Dependencies
- None (this is the foundation phase)

## References
- Architecture review: git add -f staging block is highest-risk missed change
- Architecture review: .gitattributes memory/ prefix is critical for correct gitattribute scoping
- Security review: Remove -f from git add (M4) — can silently stage files user wants private
- Security review: Use mkdir -m 700 for memory directory (L2) — knowledge.jsonl may contain sensitive info
- Simplicity review: Do NOT add LAVRA_DIR variable — inline the path, no abstraction for local use
title: Phase 1: .lavra/ skeleton and provision-memory update

description:
## What
Update all installer and uninstaller scripts to provision .lavra/ instead of .beads/memory/, and add auto-migration logic that copies existing .beads/ Lavra data to .lavra/ on detection. Covers Claude, OpenCode, Gemini, and Cortex installers.

## Context
The installers live in installers/. Each platform installer calls provision-memory.sh (already updated in Phase 1) to set up the memory system. The migration function must: detect existing Lavra data in .beads/, copy it safely to .lavra/, and exclude stale cached files.

## Call Order (CRITICAL)
Migration MUST run BEFORE provision in all installers:
1. migrate_beads_to_lavra "$TARGET"   ← FIRST
2. provision_memory_dir "$TARGET" ...  ← SECOND

If provision runs first, it creates an empty .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl. The migration's idempotency guard checks for non-empty knowledge.jsonl — a provision-created empty file would satisfy the guard and skip migration entirely, leaving all data in .beads/ silently. The order is load-bearing.

## Migration Function (full spec)

```bash
migrate_beads_to_lavra() {
  local TARGET="$1"
  local migrated=0

  # Idempotent: skip if .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl is non-empty (has data)
  # NOTE: check for non-empty (-s flag) not just present — provision creates an empty file
  if [[ -s "$TARGET/.lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl" ]]; then
    echo "  [i] .lavra/ already has knowledge data -- skipping migration"
    return 0
  fi

  if [ -d "$TARGET/.beads/memory" ] && [ -f "$TARGET/.beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl" ]; then
    # provision_memory_dir MUST have run before this line.
    # It writes new recall.sh and knowledge-db.sh to .lavra/memory/.
    # cp -n will not overwrite them (no-clobber). Order is load-bearing.
    # Exclude SQLite cache (may have open WAL state), stale config files,
    # and scripts already written by provision.
    for f in "$TARGET/.beads/memory/"*; do
      [ -L "$f" ] && continue  # skip symlinks — don't dereference
      fname=$(basename "$f")
      case "$fname" in
        *.db|*.db-wal|*.db-shm|*.db-journal|.gitattributes|.gitignore) continue ;;
        recall.sh|knowledge-db.sh) continue ;;  # new versions already written by provisioning
        *) cp -n "$f" "$TARGET/.lavra/memory/" 2>/dev/null || true ;;
      esac
    done
    migrated=1
  fi

  if [ -d "$TARGET/.beads/config" ] && [ -f "$TARGET/.beads/config/lavra.json" ]; then
    # Copy all config files — .beads/config/ only contains lavra-owned files
    cp -rn "$TARGET/.beads/config/." "$TARGET/.lavra/config/" 2>/dev/null || true
    migrated=1
  fi

  if [ -d "$TARGET/.beads/retros" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$TARGET/.lavra/retros"
    # mkdir -p ensures destination exists before copy (cp -rn requires dest dir to exist)
    cp -rn "$TARGET/.beads/retros/." "$TARGET/.lavra/retros/" 2>/dev/null || true
    migrated=1
  fi

  if [ "$migrated" -eq 1 ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "[!] Old Lavra data found in .beads/ — copied to .lavra/"
    echo "    .beads/ data preserved. Safe to delete after verifying .lavra/ data is intact."
    echo "    To remove: rm -rf .beads/memory .beads/config/lavra.json .beads/retros"
    echo ""
  fi
}
```

Removed from function vs original plan:
- Path traversal validation: REMOVED — installers already call resolve_target_dir() from shared-functions.sh which canonicalizes TARGET before migration is reached. Re-validating inside the function is duplicate defensive programming.
- config/ selective exclusion logic: REMOVED — .beads/config/ only contains lavra-owned files (lavra.json, project-setup.md, codebase-profile.md). No exclusion logic needed; use cp -rn directly.
- Post-copy line count integrity check: REMOVED — cp -n either succeeds or fails; it does not silently truncate. A line count check after successful cp verifies nothing meaningful.

Added vs original plan:
- [ -L "$f" ] && continue: skip symlinks during memory/ copy to avoid dereferencing paths outside .beads/
- -s flag on idempotency guard: check non-empty (-s) not just present (-f) to handle provision-created empty file
- cp -rn for config/ directory (not selective): simplified to single bulk copy

## Specific Changes Per Installer

### install-claude.sh
1. Update mkdir -p path from .beads/config → .lavra/config (belt-and-suspenders line)
2. Update messaging to .lavra/memory/knowledge.jsonl and .lavra/memory/recall.sh
3. Add call: migrate_beads_to_lavra "$TARGET" BEFORE provision_memory_dir call

### install-opencode.sh, install-gemini.sh, install-cortex.sh
Same three changes as install-claude.sh for each.

### uninstall-claude.sh, uninstall-cortex.sh
1. Update messaging from .beads/ → .lavra/ (what Lavra data is preserved)
2. Add: 'To fully remove Lavra data: rm -rf .lavra/'

### uninstall-opencode.sh, uninstall-gemini.sh
Update paths for recall.sh, knowledge-db.sh, knowledge.jsonl removal from .beads/memory/ → .lavra/memory/.

## Decisions

### Locked
- migrate_beads_to_lavra() called BEFORE provision_memory_dir() in all 4 platform installers
- Idempotency guard uses -s (non-empty) not -f (present)
- No path traversal re-validation inside migration function (handled by installer's resolve_target_dir())
- config/ copy is bulk cp -rn (no exclusion logic)
- No post-copy integrity check
- Skip symlinks in memory/ copy loop with [ -L ] check

### Discretion
- Exact wording of migration warning message
- Whether migration function lives in shared-functions.sh or is inlined per installer

## Testing
- [ ] Fresh install: .lavra/ created, no .beads/memory/ by Lavra
- [ ] Migration scenario: simulate existing .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl with test data, run installer, verify knowledge.jsonl copied to .lavra/memory/ with same content
- [ ] Migration idempotency: run installer twice — second run must not overwrite .lavra/ data
- [ ] Migration with empty .beads/memory/: must not error
- [ ] Verify .beads/ data is preserved (not moved/deleted) after migration
- [ ] Verify SQLite files (.db, .db-wal, .db-shm, .db-journal) NOT copied to .lavra/
- [ ] Verify recall.sh and knowledge-db.sh in .lavra/ are the NEW versions (from provision), not old ones from .beads/
- [ ] Verify config/ copied (lavra.json present in .lavra/config/)
- [ ] Verify retros/ copied if present
- [ ] Verify migration warning message printed when data detected

## Validation
- [ ] migrate_beads_to_lavra() called BEFORE provision_memory_dir() in all 4 installers
- [ ] Idempotency guard checks -s (non-empty), not -f (present)
- [ ] No path traversal validation inside migration function (not needed)
- [ ] config/ uses cp -rn bulk copy (no exclusion logic)
- [ ] No post-copy line count check
- [ ] Symlinks in .beads/memory/ are skipped (not dereferenced)

## Files
- installers/install-claude.sh
- installers/install-opencode.sh
- installers/install-gemini.sh
- installers/install-cortex.sh
- installers/uninstall-claude.sh
- installers/uninstall-opencode.sh
- installers/uninstall-gemini.sh
- installers/uninstall-cortex.sh

## Dependencies
- bdcompound-63c.2 (Phase 1) must complete first
- bdcompound-63c.1 (Phase 2) should complete first (provision_memory_dir path updated)

## References
- Architecture review: call order is load-bearing — migrate first, then provision
- Architecture review: idempotency guard must check -s not -f
- Simplicity review: remove path traversal validation (duplicate), config exclusion (YAGNI), integrity check (theater)
- Security review: M3 (symlink traversal) — skip symlinks in copy loop with -L check

description:
## What
Update all command .md files, optional commands, agent .md files, skill files to reference .lavra/ paths instead of .beads/ paths. Platform-converted copies (opencode/, gemini/, cortex/) are handled by re-running the conversion scripts after updating canonical sources — NOT by manually patching generated output.

## Context
Commands live in plugins/lavra/commands/ and plugins/lavra/commands/optional/. Agents in plugins/lavra/agents/. Skills in plugins/lavra/skills/.
CRITICAL: platform-converted copies exist in plugins/lavra/opencode/, plugins/lavra/gemini/, plugins/lavra/cortex/ — 82+ files total. These are GENERATED OUTPUT. Do NOT manually sed them. Re-run conversion scripts after updating canonical sources.

## Approach: Three Steps

### Step 1: Scoped sed pass on CANONICAL SOURCES only
Run over commands/, commands/optional/, agents/, skills/ — replacing ONLY the three Lavra-owned subpaths:

```bash
find plugins/lavra/commands plugins/lavra/commands/optional      plugins/lavra/agents plugins/lavra/skills      -name "*.md" -o -name "*.toml" |   xargs sed -i     -e 's|\.beads/memory/|.lavra/memory/|g'     -e 's|\.beads/config/|.lavra/config/|g'     -e 's|\.beads/retros/|.lavra/retros/|g'
```

SCOPE: Only memory/, config/, retros/ subpaths — NOT .beads/ globally. This preserves:
- .beads/backup/ in lavra-ship.md (beads CLI owned — DO NOT CHANGE)
- Any prose describing the beads CLI itself

### Step 2: Regenerate platform copies by re-running conversion scripts
After Step 1 updates canonical sources, regenerate all platform-converted files:

```bash
bun run scripts/convert-opencode.ts
bun run scripts/convert-gemini.ts
# Run cortex converter if it exists
```

This eliminates all .beads/ refs in opencode/, gemini/, cortex/ in one operation AND keeps conversion scripts as the single source of truth. If conversion scripts are re-run in the future (for model updates, format changes), they will produce correct .lavra/ output because the source templates are correct.

WHY NOT MANUAL SED ON PLATFORM COPIES: Running sed on generated output creates drift. The next time conversion scripts run (model update, format change), the new output will re-introduce .beads/ paths unless the canonical sources were updated — which they are in Step 1. Option B (re-run converters) is architecturally correct; Option A (manual sed on generated files) creates a maintenance hazard.

BEFORE RUNNING: Check conversion scripts for hardcoded .beads/ output paths:
```bash
grep '\.beads/' scripts/convert-opencode.ts scripts/convert-gemini.ts
```
If the scripts themselves contain hardcoded .beads/ paths, update them first before re-running.

### Step 3: Grep verification (acceptance test)
```bash
# Must return zero results (Lavra-owned paths only):
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/commands/
grep -r '\.beads/config' plugins/lavra/commands/
grep -r '\.beads/retros' plugins/lavra/commands/
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/agents/
grep -r '\.beads/config' plugins/lavra/agents/
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/skills/
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/opencode/
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/gemini/
grep -r '\.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/cortex/

# Must STILL return results (beads CLI owned — DO NOT CHANGE):
grep -r '\.beads/backup' plugins/lavra/commands/lavra-ship.md
```

## Files and Known References (canonical sources only)

### Commands with .beads/ refs:
- lavra-work.md (8 refs + XML tag + reason string)
- lavra-review.md (8 refs)
- lavra-recall.md (8 refs)
- lavra-work-ralph.md (6 refs + XML tag + reason string)
- lavra-work-teams.md (6 refs)
- lavra-design.md (5 refs)
- lavra-retro.md (4 refs including .beads/retros/)
- lavra-ship.md (2 refs — .beads/config/lavra.json only; .beads/backup/ stays)
- lavra-learn.md, lavra-checkpoint.md, lavra-brainstorm.md, lavra-plan.md, lavra-eng-review.md, lavra-research.md, lavra-quick.md (1-2 refs each)

### Agents with .beads/ refs:
- Most agents have 1-2 refs (knowledge recall or config read commands)

### Skills with .beads/ refs:
- project-setup, brainstorming, and a few others reference .beads/config/

## Decisions

### Locked
- Canonical sources updated first (sed pass)
- Platform copies regenerated via conversion scripts (NOT manual sed)
- .beads/backup/ in lavra-ship.md stays unchanged (beads CLI owned)
- Conversion scripts checked for hardcoded .beads/ paths before re-running

### Discretion
- Whether to check conversion scripts' own .beads/ refs before or after the canonical sed pass

## Testing
- [ ] Sed pass runs without error on all canonical source files
- [ ] Conversion scripts regenerate opencode/, gemini/ output successfully
- [ ] All grep verification commands return zero results
- [ ] lavra-ship.md still contains .beads/backup/ (not changed)
- [ ] Spot-check 3-4 files in opencode/ and gemini/ to confirm .lavra/ paths present

## Validation
- [ ] grep -r '.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/commands/ returns zero
- [ ] grep -r '.beads/config' plugins/lavra/commands/ returns zero
- [ ] grep -r '.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/opencode/ returns zero (after re-run)
- [ ] grep -r '.beads/memory' plugins/lavra/gemini/ returns zero (after re-run)
- [ ] lavra-ship.md .beads/backup/ still present
- [ ] Conversion scripts checked for hardcoded .beads/ output paths

## Files
- plugins/lavra/commands/*.md
- plugins/lavra/commands/optional/*.md
- plugins/lavra/agents/**/*.md
- plugins/lavra/skills/*/SKILL.md (and related files)
- plugins/lavra/opencode/ (regenerated, not manually patched)
- plugins/lavra/gemini/ (regenerated, not manually patched)
- plugins/lavra/cortex/ (regenerated if converter exists)
- scripts/convert-opencode.ts (check for hardcoded .beads/ paths, update if found)
- scripts/convert-gemini.ts (check for hardcoded .beads/ paths, update if found)

## Dependencies
- bdcompound-63c.2 (Phase 1) and bdcompound-63c.1 (Phase 2) must complete first

## References
- Architecture review: re-run conversion scripts (Option B) not manual sed on generated files (Option A)
- Simplicity review: Option B eliminates 506+ occurrences of drift risk permanently
- Architecture review: check conversion scripts for hardcoded .beads/ paths before re-running

Update all documentation, CLAUDE.md rules, site/ docs, CI workflows, pre-release-check.sh, and README to reference .lavra/ paths consistently.

### Site documentation (site/src/content/docs/)

### scripts/pre-release-check.sh

## Key Changes

### github-release.md (critical — used in release process)
Update ALL smoke test commands:
- `ls .beads/memory/` → `ls .lavra/memory/`
- All path verification commands in "Verify the memory directory:" sections

IMPORTANT STRUCTURAL CHANGE: .gitignore and .gitattributes are now at .lavra/ root, NOT .lavra/memory/. Smoke tests must check:
```bash
ls /tmp/test-beads-install/.lavra/
# Must contain: .gitignore, .gitattributes, .lavra-version, memory/, config/, retros/
```

description:
Refactor install.sh and uninstall.sh to be thin dispatchers that route to platform-specific installer scripts.

## Architecture

Main scripts become routers:
- install.sh: Parse flags, default to -claude, exec installers/install-<platform>.sh
- uninstall.sh: Parse flags, default to -claude, exec installers/uninstall-<platform>.sh

## Structure

```
installers/
├── install-claude.sh      # Current install.sh logic (541 lines)
├── install-opencode.sh    # .opencode/plugins/, commands/, skills/, agents/
├── install-gemini.sh      # Gemini extension files
├── uninstall-claude.sh    # Current uninstall.sh logic
├── uninstall-opencode.sh  # Remove .opencode/ files
└── uninstall-gemini.sh    # Remove Gemini extension
```

## Implementation

### 1. Move existing logic
```bash
# Move current install.sh to installers/install-claude.sh
mv install.sh installers/install-claude.sh
mv uninstall.sh installers/uninstall-claude.sh
```

### 2. Create thin dispatchers
install.sh:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
PLATFORM="claude"  # Default for backwards compatibility

case "$1" in
  -claude|--claude)
    PLATFORM="claude"
    shift
    ;;
  -opencode|--opencode)
    PLATFORM="opencode"
    shift
    ;;
  -gemini|--gemini)
    PLATFORM="gemini"
    shift
    ;;
  -h|--help)
    echo "Usage: ./install.sh [-claude|-opencode|-gemini] [options]"
    echo "Default: -claude"
    exit 0
    ;;
esac

exec "$(dirname "$0")/installers/install-${PLATFORM}.sh" "$@"
```

uninstall.sh follows same pattern.

### 3. Create OpenCode installer (blocked by bdcompound-xr3)
installers/install-opencode.sh:
- Copy plugin.ts + package.json to .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
- Run bun install
- Copy hooks/
- Copy commands/ to .opencode/commands/ (once available)
- Copy skills/ to .opencode/skills/ (once available)
- Copy agents/ to .opencode/agents/ (once available)
- Security: validate .opencode/ is not a symlink
- Set restrictive permissions

### 4. Create Gemini installer
installers/install-gemini.sh:
- Instructions to run: gemini extensions install <github-url>
- Or manual: copy gemini-extension.json, gemini/settings.json
- Copy hooks/ if manual install

## Benefits

- Clean separation of platform logic
- Easy to add new platforms (just add new script)
- Each installer testable independently
- No complex branching in main scripts
- Backwards compatible (default to -claude)

## Testing

```bash
# Should work as before (default Claude)
./install.sh
./install.sh /path/to/project

# New platform flags
./install.sh -opencode
./install.sh -gemini
./uninstall.sh -opencode /path/to/project
```
notes:
## SECURITY REQUIREMENTS (from plan review)

### Critical Security Controls

1. **Platform Flag Validation**
   - Use strict allowlist validation (claude|opencode|gemini only)
   - Prevent command injection via platform flag
   - Implementation:
     ```bash
     validate_platform() {
       local platform="$1"
       case "$platform" in
         claude|opencode|gemini) return 0 ;;
         *)
           echo "[\!] Error: Invalid platform '$platform'"
           echo "    Allowed: claude, opencode, gemini"
           exit 1
           ;;
       esac
     }
     ```

2. **Installer Path Security**
   - Verify installer exists and is in expected directory
   - Use path canonicalization:
     ```bash
     INSTALLER="$SCRIPT_DIR/installers/install-${PLATFORM}.sh"
     INSTALLER_REAL="$(realpath "$INSTALLER")"
     EXPECTED_DIR="$(realpath "$SCRIPT_DIR/installers")"
     
     if [[ \! "$INSTALLER_REAL" =~ ^"$EXPECTED_DIR"/install-[a-z]+\.sh$ ]]; then
       echo "[\!] Error: Installer path validation failed"
       exit 1
     fi
     ```

3. **Directory Creation Safety**
   - Prevent TOCTOU race conditions
   - Validate ownership and symlink checks:
     ```bash
     create_safe_dir() {
       local dir="$1"
       mkdir -m 700 "$dir" || return 1
       
       # Verify not a symlink
       if [[ -L "$dir" ]]; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Directory is a symlink"
         return 1
       fi
       
       # Verify ownership
       local owner=$(stat -f%Su "$dir" 2>/dev/null || stat -c%U "$dir")
       if [[ "$owner" \!= "$USER" ]]; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Directory owned by different user"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     ```

4. **File Copy Validation**
   - Allowlist file types before copy
   - Scan for suspicious patterns
   - No executables in data directories:
     ```bash
     safe_copy_directory() {
       local src="$1" dst="$2"
       mkdir -p "$dst"
       
       # Copy only approved file types
       find "$src" -type f \( -name "*.md" -o -name "*.json" -o -name "*.yaml" \) \
         -exec cp {} "$dst/" \;
       
       # Verify no executables
       if find "$dst" -type f -perm +111 | grep -q .; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Executable files detected"
         rm -rf "$dst"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     ```

5. **MCP Config Merge Security**
   - Validate JSON structure before merge
   - Check for prototype pollution (__proto__, constructor)
   - Backup existing config:
     ```bash
     validate_mcp_config() {
       local config="$1"
       
       # Check for prototype pollution
       if echo "$config" | jq -e '.mcpServers | has("__proto__")' | grep -q true; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Prototype pollution detected"
         return 1
       fi
       
       # Validate structure
       if \! echo "$config" | jq -e '.mcpServers | type == "object"' > /dev/null; then
         echo "[\!] Error: Invalid MCP config structure"
         return 1
       fi
     }
     
     # Backup before merge
     cp "$target_config" "${target_config}.backup"
     ```

6. **Permission Hardening**
   - Set restrictive umask: `umask 077`
   - Explicit permissions after file operations:
     ```bash
     # Commands: read-only
     find "$COMMANDS_DIR" -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;
     
     # Hooks: executable
     find "$HOOKS_DIR" -type f -name "*.sh" -exec chmod 755 {} \;
     
     # Skills: read-only
     find "$SKILLS_DIR" -type f -name "SKILL.md" -exec chmod 444 {} \;
     ```

### Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Platform flag allowlist validation
- [ ] Installer path canonicalization check
- [ ] Safe directory creation (TOCTOU protection)
- [ ] File type allowlist for copies
- [ ] MCP config validation and backup
- [ ] Explicit file permissions (644/755)
- [ ] Umask set to 077 before operations

### Testing Requirements

- [ ] Test invalid platform flags (../../../etc/passwd)
- [ ] Test symlink directory attacks
- [ ] Test malicious MCP configs (prototype pollution)
- [ ] Verify file permissions after install

description:
## What

Add a FACT knowledge entry documenting how Astro 6's glob loader normalizes collection entry IDs.

## Context

Discovered during docs sidebar fix (commit 824ca14). The sidebar was showing empty Configuration and Releases sections because the section slugs used uppercase names like 'AUTONOMOUS_EXECUTION' and 'releases/v0.7.0', but Astro 6's glob loader normalizes IDs to lowercase and also strips dots from the path (not just the .md extension).

Examples:
- AUTONOMOUS_EXECUTION.md → id: autonomous_execution
- COST.md → id: cost
- releases/v0.7.0.md → id: releases/v070 (dots stripped from the whole id, not just extension)

This is different from what the code comments said ('Astro 5 glob loader uses id (filename without extension)') — the dot-stripping behavior is Astro 6-specific and undocumented.

## What

Run this command to add the knowledge entry:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "FACT: Astro 6 glob loader normalizes collection entry IDs: lowercased + dots stripped (not just .md extension). UPPERCASE.md → uppercase. releases/v0.7.0.md → releases/v070. Section slug arrays in Doc.astro and index.astro must use lowercase ids with underscores (not hyphens) matching the loader output. Discovered when sidebar showed empty Configuration/Releases sections."
```

## Decisions

### Locked
- Entry type must be FACT (hard constraint about Astro 6 behavior, not a design choice)
- Tags should include: astro, docs, sidebar, glob-loader

### Discretion
- Wording of the entry

## Testing
- [ ] Entry appears in `recall.sh "astro"` output
- [ ] Entry type is fact

## Validation
- [ ] `bd comments add` command runs without error
- [ ] Entry is searchable via recall.sh

## Files
- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl (written by hook automatically)

## Dependencies
None — standalone knowledge capture

description:
## What

Add a PATTERN knowledge entry consolidating the config/security boundary doctrine that was established across ~9 decision entries this week but never captured as a single searchable PATTERN.

## Context

The doctrine was established across beads bdcompound-9f5, bdcompound-dnv, and others:
- Where data lives: .beads/config/ for settings (not .beads/memory/ — merge=union corrupts YAML)
- How agent context injection works safely: XML wrapping + role prefix sanitization + bidirectional char stripping
- How knowledge search is done safely: grep -iF (fixed-string) not grep -i (regex — metachar injection risk)
- Security model: anything auto-injected into agent context is untrusted, must be sanitized and tagged

The pattern is settled doctrine. It should be one searchable entry, not 9 scattered decisions.

## What

Run this command:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "PATTERN: Config/security boundary doctrine for Lavra. (1) Storage: .beads/config/ for settings, .beads/memory/ for knowledge — never mix them, merge=union in memory/ corrupts YAML frontmatter. (2) Agent context injection: always XML-wrap with <untrusted-knowledge> tag + strip role prefixes (SYSTEM:/ASSISTANT:/[INST]) + strip bidirectional override chars + strip null bytes before injection. (3) Safe search: grep -iF (fixed-string) not grep -i — prevents metacharacter injection from knowledge content. Applies to: auto-recall.sh, any new hook that reads .beads/ content."
```

## Decisions

### Locked
- Entry type must be PATTERN
- Must cover all three aspects: storage separation, injection sanitization, safe search

### Discretion
- Exact wording

## Testing

- [ ] `recall.sh "config memory"` returns this entry
- [ ] Entry type is pattern

## Validation
- [ ] Single entry replaces the need to search 9 decision entries for this doctrine
- [ ] Tags include: security, config, pattern, injection, sanitization

- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl (written by hook automatically)

title: Add PATTERN entry: config/security boundary doctrine

description:
## Issue

The planned PATTERN entry for config/security boundary doctrine should include two known limitations of the current sanitization implementation.

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — Documenting known limitations prevents false confidence in the security guarantees.

## Location
.beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl — the PATTERN entry created by bead 7ji.2

## Why This Matters
Security-sentinel found two gaps in auto-recall.sh's sanitization:

1. **Newline-in-JSON gap**: SANITIZED_KNOWLEDGE isn't newline-escaped before JSON embedding (unlike ESCAPED_STATE which uses tr '\n' ' '). A knowledge entry containing '}}' on its own line could corrupt the JSON hook output structure.

2. **Bidi-strip portability**: The sed command using codepoint escapes (\x{202A}-\x{202E}) is not portable across all sed implementations. If sed silently fails on some platforms, bidi-stripping is skipped without error.

The knowledge entry should characterize these as best-effort, not guaranteed protections.

Also: 7ji.2 should document CATALOG.md's threat model accurately — it's static developer-controlled content with drift risk, not adversarial input. This distinction matters if the catalog check added in 7ji.4 ever starts parsing it as a trusted source.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] PATTERN entry notes that sanitization is defense-in-depth, not guaranteed
- [ ] Entry notes the newline-in-JSON gap by name
- [ ] Entry notes the bidi-strip is best-effort due to sed portability
- [ ] Entry accurately characterizes CATALOG.md threat model as drift, not adversarial
title: 7ji.2: Knowledge entry should note newline-in-JSON gap and bidi-strip portability limitation

description:
## What

Add a PATTERN knowledge entry (and update CLAUDE.md guidance if appropriate) about when to use PATTERN: type explicitly vs burying patterns in DECISION/LEARNED entries.

## Context

The retro found 0 explicit PATTERN: type entries this week despite 7 entries auto-tagged 'pattern' from content. The tags come from keyword detection in memory-capture.sh, but the entry type stayed 'decision' or 'learned'. This makes it harder to filter patterns specifically and defeats the purpose of having distinct knowledge types.

The six types each have a purpose:
- LEARNED: insight from debugging/surprise behavior — one-time discovery
- DECISION: what was chosen and why — specific choice with rationale
- FACT: hard constraint, version req, environment detail — static truth
- PATTERN: recurring convention or idiom — should be reused everywhere
- INVESTIGATION: root cause analysis
- DEVIATION: auto-fix outside bead scope

When something is worth reusing as a convention (not just recording a one-time choice), it should be PATTERN.

## What

1. Add this as a PATTERN entry itself:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "PATTERN: Use PATTERN: type when capturing something meant to be reused as a convention — not just recording a one-time decision. Signal: if you'd want a future agent to do the same thing by default, it's a PATTERN. If you're recording why you made a specific choice, it's a DECISION. Examples of PATTERN: 'always XML-wrap injected content', 'use grep -iF for safe search'. Examples of DECISION: 'chose PostgreSQL over SQLite for this project', 'dropped filesystem scanning due to security risk'."
```

2. Optionally update the knowledge type descriptions in CLAUDE.md or hooks-system.md rule to add a 'When to use PATTERN vs DECISION' note.

## Decisions

### Locked
- The knowledge entry itself must use PATTERN: type (meta-example)

### Discretion
- Whether to also update CLAUDE.md/hooks-system.md rule (assess if it adds clarity)

## Testing
- [ ] Entry is type 'pattern'
- [ ] `recall.sh "pattern type"` returns this entry

## Validation
- [ ] Entry clearly distinguishes PATTERN from DECISION with concrete examples

## Files
- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl
- .claude/rules/hooks-system.md (optional — only if adding a clarifying note)

## Dependencies
None — but semantically follows bead 1 and 2 (do those first to practice the pattern)
title: Fix PATTERN type gap: add guidance to use PATTERN: prefix explicitly

description:
## Issue

The proposed PATTERN entry in bead 7ji.3 uses easy, obvious examples (PostgreSQL vs SQLite). The real confusion in the knowledge base is between LEARNED and PATTERN, and between instance-level DECISION and convention-level PATTERN.

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — The guidance will be more useful if it shows the actual borderline cases that cause agents to miscategorize.

## Why This Matters
Pattern-recognition-specialist found three real examples of miscategorization in the knowledge base:
- learned-command-naming-convention-uses-beads-prefix-for-all-workflow — this is a PATTERN filed as LEARNED
- decision-renamed-deepen-plan-to-beads-deepen-for-consistency — instance filed as DECISION, but the naming convention is the PATTERN
- decision-command-name-is-beads-design-follows-beads-naming-convention — same pattern again as DECISION

The proposed entry should include at least one real pair: 'use beads- prefix for all workflow commands' (PATTERN) vs. 'renamed /deepen-plan to /beads-deepen for consistency' (DECISION). This pair illustrates how one PATTERN generates multiple DECISION instances.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Entry includes at least one borderline example that isn't obviously one type or the other
- [ ] Entry clarifies when something is a PATTERN vs when it's a DECISION recording application of that pattern
- [ ] Entry addresses the LEARNED vs PATTERN overlap (see companion suggestion below)

## Testing Steps
1. Review the final knowledge entry wording
2. Check that the examples would have prevented the three real miscategorizations found in the knowledge base
title: 7ji.3: PATTERN vs DECISION examples should use borderline cases from actual knowledge base

description:
## Issue

The LEARNED type description in hooks-system.md is broad enough to absorb PATTERNs. 'Key technical insight discovered during work' fits naming conventions just as well as debugging surprises, so agents file recurring conventions as LEARNED rather than PATTERN.

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — Without tightening the LEARNED description, the PATTERN type gap that bead 7ji.3 is trying to close will reappear.

## Location
.claude/rules/hooks-system.md — Knowledge types section

## Why This Matters
The pattern-recognition-specialist recommends adding 'one-time discovery that surprised you, not a rule you'd apply again' to the LEARNED description. This emphasis on surprise and non-repeatability pushes conventions out of LEARNED into PATTERN.

Both hooks-system.md (normative reference) AND a knowledge entry (contextual recall) should carry this guidance — they serve different populations with different lifecycles.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] LEARNED description in hooks-system.md includes the 'surprise + non-repeatable' qualifier
- [ ] PATTERN description is updated to contrast clearly with LEARNED
- [ ] The update is one sentence per type, not a paragraph (brevity in normative docs)

## Testing Steps
1. Read updated type descriptions in hooks-system.md
2. Apply the 'surprise + non-repeatable' test to the three real miscategorizations:
   - learned-command-naming-convention... → should now be recognized as PATTERN
   - etc.
title: 7ji.3: Add LEARNED vs PATTERN disambiguation to hooks-system.md type descriptions

description:
## What

Add a script check and CI gate that cross-references CATALOG.md against actual command files in plugins/lavra/commands/, so ghost commands and missing commands are caught automatically rather than by manual inspection.

## Context

During this week's docs work, the catalog was found to have:
- 7 ghost commands (listed in catalog but file doesn't exist): /lfg, /lavra-compound, /create-agent-skill, /deploy-docs, /release-docs, /resolve-pr-parallel, /resolve-todo-parallel
- 5 missing commands (file exists but not in catalog): /lavra-ship, /lavra-qa, /lavra-retro, /lavra-learn, /lavra-ceo-review
- Wrong counts ('24 core' when actual is 23)

The release rules (github-release.md) now include a manual check step, but an automated script would catch this earlier and without relying on human discipline.

## What

Extend scripts/pre-release-check.sh with a command catalog accuracy check:

1. Extract command names listed in CATALOG.md (parse the table rows, extract /command-name entries)
2. List actual command files: ls plugins/lavra/commands/*.md + plugins/lavra/commands/optional/*.md
3. Compare: report commands in catalog but missing as files (ghosts), and files missing from catalog
4. Fail pre-release check if any ghosts found (file must exist for a command to be listed)
5. Warn (not fail) for missing from catalog (new commands may be intentionally unlisted during dev)

Also add the same check to .github/workflows/test-installation.yml verify-release job to keep them in sync (per the SYNC: convention both files share).

## Decisions

### Locked
- Ghost commands (in catalog, no file) = hard failure — never ship docs for commands that don't exist
- Missing from catalog (file exists, not in catalog) = warning only — may be intentional during dev
- Must keep pre-release-check.sh and CI yaml in sync (they have SYNC: comments pointing to each other)
- Use bash/grep/awk only — no new dependencies

### Discretion
- Exact parsing approach for CATALOG.md (grep for /lavra- pattern in table rows is simplest)
- Whether to check PLATFORMS.md feature flags (harder to automate, lower value — probably skip)

## Testing
- [ ] Script detects a ghost command when one is temporarily added to CATALOG.md
- [ ] Script detects a missing command when a new .md file is added to commands/ without updating catalog
- [ ] CI job runs the check and fails on ghost commands
- [ ] pre-release-check.sh and CI check use identical logic

## Validation
- [ ] `bash scripts/pre-release-check.sh` fails if CATALOG.md lists a command with no corresponding .md file
- [ ] `bash scripts/pre-release-check.sh` warns (exit 0) if a command file exists but isn't in CATALOG.md
- [ ] Both files have updated SYNC: comments referencing each other

## Files
- scripts/pre-release-check.sh
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml

## Dependencies
None — standalone script change
title: Automate docs accuracy check in pre-release

description:
## Issue

Bead 7ji.4 plans to add the CATALOG.md accuracy check to pre-release-check.sh and the verify-release CI job. The verify-release job only runs on release events — catalog drift would silently merge into main and only surface when a release is tagged.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — By the time the release gate fires, the PR has already merged. Developers see a preventable release failure that requires a patch tag or reverted release.

## Location
.github/workflows/test-installation.yml — verify-release job (release-only trigger)

## Why This Matters
The intent of bead 7ji.4 is to catch ghost commands before they ship. If the check only runs at release time, the drift window is the entire development sprint. A developer adding a command in the middle of a sprint gets no signal until they push a release tag.

The test-compatibility job runs on every PR against main — that is the right gate for content accuracy.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Catalog accuracy check runs on every PR to main (not just release events)
- [ ] The verify-release job still confirms catalog accuracy as a final gate
- [ ] Developer gets CI failure at PR time when they add a command without updating CATALOG.md

## Testing Steps
1. Add a new command file to plugins/lavra/commands/ without updating CATALOG.md
2. Open a PR against main
3. Expected: CI fails with missing-from-catalog error (not just at release time)
title: Catalog accuracy check must run on PR CI, not just release gate

description:
## Issue

Bead 7ji.4 plans to WARN (exit 0) for commands that exist as files but are missing from CATALOG.md. For a published marketplace plugin, undocumented commands are a defect — users cannot discover or use features that are not in the catalog.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Silent undocumented features ship to users who have no way to know they exist. The CATALOG.md is the primary user-facing reference.

## Location
scripts/pre-release-check.sh — planned catalog accuracy check section

## Why This Matters
The asymmetry argument (ghost = worse than missing) is correct, but it does not justify silently shipping undocumented features. The github-release.md checklist already requires manual CATALOG.md updates before release. Automated enforcement should match the checklist intent.

A WARN under release pressure is easily missed. By the time it surfaces, undocumented commands have shipped to installed users.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] pre-release-check.sh exits 1 when any command file is missing from CATALOG.md
- [ ] Error message names the specific missing command file
- [ ] Developers can intentionally exclude commands from catalog by adding to an explicit allowlist, if needed

## Testing Steps
1. Add a new command file without updating CATALOG.md
2. Run bash scripts/pre-release-check.sh
3. Expected: exits 1 with clear error naming the missing command
title: Missing-from-catalog should fail, not warn (published marketplace)

description:
## Issue

CATALOG.md contains command names in two contexts: table rows (the authoritative listing) and prose descriptions (e.g., 'Included in `/lavra-design`'). A naive grep for `/command-name` across the whole document will produce false positives from prose mentions.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — False positive ghost detections would cause the pre-release check to fail for commands that ARE properly listed in the catalog table.

## Location
scripts/pre-release-check.sh — catalog accuracy check implementation

## Why This Matters
CATALOG.md descriptions mention other commands inline. Without constraining extraction to table rows (lines starting with `|`), the check would report commands that are mentioned in descriptions as ghost entries.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Command extraction uses a pattern that matches only table rows (pipe-delimited lines)
- [ ] No false positives from command names appearing in prose descriptions
- [ ] Recommended: grep -E '^\|' to filter to table rows before extracting command names

## Testing Steps
1. Add a prose mention of '/lavra-design' in a CATALOG.md description column
2. Run the catalog check
3. Expected: no ghost entry for /lavra-design (it correctly reads from the table row, not the prose mention)
title: Grep for catalog commands must be constrained to table rows only

description:

- [ ] WARN-level findings appear in both the check output and the summary count

title: Add WARN counter to pre-release-check.sh summary line

description:

When the catalog check detects a ghost command (listed in CATALOG.md but no corresponding file), the FAIL message should name the specific ghost, not just report a generic failure.

## Severity

- [ ] Developer can immediately identify what to fix without additional investigation

title: Ghost FAIL messages should name the specific ghost command

description:
## Issue

Both pre-release-check.sh and the CI verify-release job must have identical catalog logic (per the SYNC: comment pattern). Every new catalog check adds to the SYNC maintenance burden. A separate validate-docs.sh would let CI call the script directly, eliminating duplication.

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — The SYNC pattern works but creates ongoing maintenance risk. A dedicated script is the cleaner long-term path.

## Location
scripts/pre-release-check.sh, .github/workflows/test-installation.yml

## Why This Matters
The architecture-strategist noted: extracting to validate-docs.sh would:
1. Let CI call the script directly (eliminating SYNC debt for all docs checks)
2. Keep pre-release-check.sh scoped to assembly validation (versions, counts, conversion outputs)
3. Allow the script to expand to cover agents.md vs. agent files, skills.md vs. skill dirs

This is the path that avoids accumulating more SYNC debt as more docs accuracy checks are added.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] validate-docs.sh covers CATALOG.md vs. commands at minimum
- [ ] CI calls validate-docs.sh instead of duplicating the logic
- [ ] pre-release-check.sh calls validate-docs.sh (or CI calls both separately)

## Testing Steps
1. Create validate-docs.sh with the catalog check
2. Update CI to call the script
3. Verify pre-release-check.sh and CI produce the same results from the same source
title: Consider extracting catalog validation to validate-docs.sh to eliminate SYNC debt

description:
## What

Add a knowledge entry and optionally a CLAUDE.md note clarifying when to use chore: vs other conventional commit prefixes, specifically for the infra/config changes that tend to slip through as untyped.

## Context

The CNAME commit this week was untyped: 'add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain' should have been 'chore: add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain'. Low stakes individually, but untyped commits break changelog generation and git log --grep filtering.

The ambiguous cases are:
- DNS/domain changes → chore:
- CI config changes → chore: or ci: (either fine, ci: preferred)
- Dependency bumps → chore:
- Gitignore/gitattributes → chore:
- README-only → docs:
- Script/tool additions → chore: (if not user-facing) or feat: (if user-facing)

## What

Add a FACT entry:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "FACT: Conventional commit type for infra/config-only changes: use chore: for DNS records, CI config, gitignore, gitattributes, dependency bumps, non-user-facing scripts. Use ci: for CI-specific changes (either is fine but ci: is more discoverable). These are the cases most likely to slip through as untyped commits."
```

Optionally add a one-liner to CLAUDE.md global instructions if the untyped pattern recurs.

## Decisions

### Locked
- Entry type: FACT (concrete rule, not a design choice)

### Discretion
- Whether to add to CLAUDE.md (only worth it if this is a recurring mistake)

## Testing
- [ ] Entry searchable via `recall.sh "chore commit"`

## Validation
- [ ] Tags include: git, commit, convention, chore

## Files
- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl
- ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (optional)

## Dependencies
None
title: Document chore: commit convention for infra/config changes

description:
## Issue
execSync calls in bin/plan-export.js and bin/plan-view.js interpolate bead IDs directly into shell commands without validation. A bead ID containing shell metacharacters could execute arbitrary commands.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Command injection via user-controlled input

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js: execSync(`bd show ${beadId}`) pattern
- bin/plan-view.js: execSync(`bd list --parent ${beadId}`) pattern

## Why This Matters
Any user-provided argument passed to the CLI could inject shell commands. While bead IDs are typically auto-generated, users can pass arbitrary strings as arguments.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] bead ID validated against /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ before shell interpolation
- [ ] OR use execFileSync with array args instead of execSync with template strings
- [ ] Malicious input like 'BD-001; rm -rf /' rejected or safely passed

## Testing Steps
1. Run: bd-plan-view 'BD-001; echo INJECTED'
2. Expected: validation error, not command execution
title: Command injection in plan-export.js and plan-view.js via unvalidated bead IDs

description:
## Issue
In .github/workflows/test-installation.yml, `node $GITHUB_WORKSPACE/bin/install.js` is unquoted. If a self-hosted runner has spaces in the workspace path, this splits into multiple shell tokens and fails.

## Severity
P2 — Latent failure on self-hosted runners

## Location
.github/workflows/test-installation.yml (npx installer job, node invocation line)

## Fix
```yaml
node "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/bin/install.js" --claude --yes
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] All $GITHUB_WORKSPACE references in shell steps are quoted
title: GITHUB_WORKSPACE unquoted in CI — breaks on self-hosted runners with spaces in path

description:
## Issue
.github/workflows/npm-publish.yml publishes without:
1. `--provenance` flag (SLSA attestation linking package to CI run/commit)
2. `--access public` flag (explicit scope for scoped packages)

## Severity
P2 — Supply chain hygiene gap; no cryptographic link from published package to source

## Location
.github/workflows/npm-publish.yml: npm publish step

## Fix
Add to job permissions: `id-token: write`
Change publish command: `npm publish --provenance --access public`

## Validation Criteria

- [ ] --access public prevents accidental private publish for scoped packages
title: npm publish missing --provenance and --access public flags

description:
## Issue
The npx smoke test job in .github/workflows/test-installation.yml runs the beads-compound installer which calls install.sh, which calls 'bd init'. The bd CLI is not installed in CI, so bd init fails with a non-zero exit and the entire installer fails.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Blocks CI, every commit fails the test suite

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: test-npx-installer job
- install.sh: bd init call

## Why This Matters
The CI gate cannot pass, making the npm-publish workflow unreliable and blocking confidence in releases.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] CI test-npx-installer job passes green
- [ ] Either install bd in CI before running the installer, OR skip bd init in non-interactive CI

## Testing Steps
1. Push to branch and observe test-installation.yml run
2. Expected: test-npx-installer passes
title: npx smoke test fails in CI — bd not installed in CI environment

close_reason: Restored process.on('exit', cleanup). Investigation showed die() calls precede setRawMode in current code, but the handler is valuable defensive practice for future changes.
description:
## Issue
The die() function in bin/plan-view.js calls process.exit(1) without first calling cleanup(). When buildTree fails (e.g., bead not found), the terminal is left in raw mode with the cursor hidden, requiring the user to run reset or stty sane to recover.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL — Corrupts the user's terminal on bead lookup failure.

## Location
- bin/plan-view.js:451 — die() calls process.exit(1) with no cleanup
- bin/plan-view.js:134 — die() called from buildTree after raw mode is already set (line 487)

## Root Cause
process.on('exit', cleanup) was removed in commit 873d9f1. Previously it was the catch-all. Now cleanup() is only called from keyboard handlers. The die() path bypasses all keyboard handlers.

## Why This Matters
Any user running bd-plan-view BD-NONEXISTENT will get a corrupted terminal. Raw mode stays on, cursor stays hidden. This is the most common error path.

## Fix Options
1. Restore process.on('exit', cleanup) (one line, no side effects)
2. Call cleanup() inside die() before process.exit(1)

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Running bd-plan-view BD-NONEXISTENT does not leave terminal in broken state
- [ ] Cursor is visible after exit
- [ ] Terminal input echoing works normally after failed lookup

## Testing Steps
1. Run: node bin/plan-view.js BD-NONEXISTENT
2. Expected: error message, terminal fully restored
3. NOT expected: cursor missing, input echoing broken
title: plan-view.js: die() doesn't call cleanup() — terminal left in broken state

description:
## Issue
In .github/workflows/test-installation.yml, the 'Run installation tests' step has continue-on-error: true. This means even when beads is available and tests actually run, a genuine test failure does not fail CI. The step also explicitly does exit 0 in its catch block, creating a double guard that guarantees CI always stays green regardless of test outcome.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL — Real installation regressions pass CI silently.

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: 'Run installation tests' step (~line 57-70)

## Why This Matters
Making a step conditional (if: steps.check-beads.outputs.available == 'true') correctly handles the optional external dependency. But adding continue-on-error: true on top means: when beads IS available and tests run, failures are still ignored. This defeats the purpose of the test step.

## Fix
Remove continue-on-error: true from the 'Run installation tests' step. The conditional guard already handles beads being unavailable. If beads is installed and the test fails, that should be a real CI failure.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Removing continue-on-error and running CI confirms green on a correct installation
- [ ] A deliberate test failure (e.g., break a directory path assertion) causes CI to go red

## Testing Steps
1. Remove continue-on-error: true from the step
2. Push and verify green run
3. Introduce deliberate failure, verify CI goes red
title: CI: continue-on-error on installation test step swallows real failures

description:
## Issue
The npm-publish workflow has no needs: verify-release dependency. This means npm publish can run and succeed while the verify-release job (which checks version consistency, component counts, and conversion outputs) is still running or has failed.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Can publish a broken or incorrectly-versioned package to npm.

## Location
- .github/workflows/npm-publish.yml: publish job has no needs: clause referencing verify-release

## Why This Matters
The verify-release job exists precisely to gate releases. Running npm-publish without waiting for it means the gate can be bypassed on any release. A version mismatch or missing conversion outputs would result in a published package that fails to install correctly.

## Fix
Add needs: [verify-release] to the publish job in npm-publish.yml. This requires that verify-release runs first and passes before npm publish executes.

Note: verify-release is in test-installation.yml which is a separate workflow. Cross-workflow dependencies may require using workflow_run trigger or a combined workflow. Evaluate the simplest approach.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] npm-publish does not run if verify-release fails
- [ ] CI order is deterministic: verify first, publish second

## Testing Steps
1. Add needs dependency
2. Create a test release tag
3. Verify verify-release runs before publish
title: npm-publish.yml: no needs:verify-release — can publish while CI verification failing

description:
## Issue
Both npx installer test jobs in test-installation.yml install the beads CLI via:
  curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash

This pins to the HEAD of the main branch, not to a specific commit SHA. If the steveyegge/beads repo is compromised, renamed, or its install script is maliciously modified, arbitrary code runs in the CI runner with access to NPM_TOKEN and other repo secrets.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Supply chain risk in CI environment.

## Location
- .github/workflows/test-installation.yml: Install beads CLI steps (~lines 55, 110)

## Why This Matters
curl|bash from a mutable URL is the canonical supply chain attack vector. The CI runner has NPM_TOKEN in scope.

## Fix Options
1. Pin to a specific commit SHA: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/<SHA>/scripts/install.sh
2. Vendor the install script into this repo and use a local path
3. Add a checksum verification step after download before execution

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] The install script URL is pinned to a specific SHA or checksum-verified
- [ ] A compromised HEAD of steveyegge/beads main does not affect this CI

## Testing Steps
1. Pin the URL to a known-good SHA
2. Verify CI still passes with the pinned URL
title: CI: curl|bash to install beads has no SHA pin — supply chain risk

description:
## Issue
bdExecFile in bin/plan-view.js uses timeout: 10000 (10 seconds) while the equivalent function in bin/plan-export.js uses timeout: 30000 (30 seconds). On a slow system or large bead tree, a 10s timeout causes bdExecFile to silently return empty string, which plan-view.js interprets as a missing bead — silently dropping phases or tasks from the displayed tree.

## Severity
P2 IMPORTANT — Silent data loss: users see an incomplete tree with no error indication.

## Location
- bin/plan-view.js:65 — timeout: 10000
- bin/plan-export.js:82 — timeout: 30000

## Why This Matters
The failure mode in plan-view.js is worse than in plan-export.js. In plan-export.js a timeout aborts with an error message. In plan-view.js the error is silently swallowed (bdExecFile returns empty string), so the TUI shows a truncated tree as if the missing beads don't exist. Users have no way to know data is missing.

## Fix
Raise timeout in plan-view.js bdExecFile to 30000ms to match plan-export.js.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Both plan-view.js and plan-export.js use timeout: 30000
- [ ] On a slow system, plan-view waits longer rather than silently truncating

## Testing Steps
1. Update timeout in plan-view.js bdExecFile to 30000
2. Verify plan-view still works normally on fast systems
title: plan-view.js: bdExecFile timeout 10s causes silent tree truncation on slow bd calls

## Issue
validateBeadId() is called at every individual fetch function (fetchBead, fetchChildren, fetchComments in plan-export.js; bdShowJson, bdListChildren, bdCommentsList in plan-view.js) instead of once at the entry point where the bead ID enters the program. This is redundant since execFileSync already prevents injection regardless, and the ID doesn't change between calls.

Additional code cleanup items in the same files:
1. plan-export.js: require statements appear after validateBeadId function definition (lines 15-16 should be at top)
2. plan-view.js: validateBeadId uses process.stderr.write instead of console.error (inconsistent with rest of file)
3. plan-view.js: usage() has no exitCode param unlike plan-export.js — callers must follow up with die() creating double-error output
4. install.js: _positional naming has underscore prefix suggesting 'unused' but is used immediately
5. plan-view.js: SIGINT handler is vestigial in raw mode (Ctrl-C comes through as keypress, not signal)

## Severity
P3 NICE-TO-HAVE — Code clarity and consistency.

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js:92, 103, 113 (redundant validateBeadId calls)
- bin/plan-view.js:75, 86, 98 (redundant validateBeadId calls)
- bin/plan-export.js:15-16 (require placement)
- bin/plan-view.js:11 (stderr.write vs console.error)
- bin/plan-view.js:455-469 (usage() no exitCode)
- bin/install.js:52 (_positional naming)
- bin/plan-view.js:501-508 (vestigial SIGINT handler)

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] validateBeadId called once at top of main execution path
- [ ] require statements grouped at top of plan-export.js
- [ ] Consistent console.error usage in validateBeadId
- [ ] usage() consistent between plan-view.js and plan-export.js

## Testing Steps

3. Verify invalid bead ID exits 1 with clear error
title: Code cleanup: validateBeadId called per-function instead of once at entry
