## Issue
In bin/install.js, the uninstall path hardcodes process.cwd() as the uninstall target, ignoring any positional path argument passed by the user.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Silent data loss: user passes a project path expecting that project to be uninstalled, but current directory gets uninstalled instead

## Location
- bin/install.js:236 — `const target = process.cwd();`

## Why This Matters
`npx beads-compound --uninstall /path/to/project` silently uninstalls from the wrong directory.

## Fix
Change the uninstall target to: `const target = TARGET_PATH || process.cwd();`

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] `npx beads-compound --uninstall /tmp/some-project` uninstalls from /tmp/some-project, not cwd
- [ ] `npx beads-compound --uninstall` (no path) still uninstalls from cwd

## Testing Steps
1. Install into /tmp/test-project
2. From a different directory, run: node bin/install.js --uninstall /tmp/test-project
3. Expected: /tmp/test-project uninstalled. cwd unchanged.
title: Uninstall in install.js always uses cwd, ignores TARGET_PATH argument

description:
## Issue
In bin/plan-export.js, bd subprocess errors cause process.exit(1) with no user-facing error message. Users see a blank failure with no indication of what went wrong.

## Severity
P1 CRITICAL - Silent failures that are impossible to debug

## Location
- bin/plan-export.js: error handling in execSync calls

## Why This Matters
When a bead ID doesn't exist or bd is not installed, the user gets no feedback. Debugging is impossible.

## Fix
Catch subprocess errors, print a descriptive message, then exit:
```js
try {
  const output = execSync(`bd show ...`);
} catch (err) {
  console.error(`Error: failed to get bead data. Is bd installed? Details: ${err.message}`);
  process.exit(1);
}
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Running bd-plan-export with a nonexistent bead shows a clear error message
- [ ] Error includes enough info to diagnose (is bd installed? does bead exist?)

## Testing Steps
1. Run: bd-plan-export BD-NONEXISTENT
2. Expected: descriptive error message, exit 1
3. NOT expected: silent exit 1 with no output
title: plan-export.js exits hard on any bd subprocess error, no error message

description:
## Issue
In bin/install.js:52-54, positional path detection uses `args.find(a => !a.startsWith('-'))`. A path like `-my-project` is silently dropped, falling through to wrong target. Also, `args.find()` is called twice redundantly.

## Severity
P2 — Silent misdirection to wrong install target

## Location
bin/install.js:52-54

## Fix
```js
const positional = args.find((a) => !a.startsWith("-"));
const TARGET_PATH = positional ? path.resolve(positional) : null;
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] args.find() called only once
- [ ] Paths starting with '-' produce a clear error rather than silent drop
title: Paths starting with '-' silently treated as flags in install.js TARGET_PATH parsing

description:
## Problem

The current workflow for thorough feature planning requires running 4 commands manually in
sequence:

1. /beads-brainstorm -- collaborative dialogue exploring WHAT to build, producing a brainstorm
bead with DECISION/INVESTIGATION/FACT/PATTERN comments
2. /beads-plan -- transforms the idea into a structured epic with child task beads, each with
detailed descriptions (What/Context/Testing/Validation/Files/Dependencies)
3. /beads-deepen -- enriches every child bead with parallel multi-agent research (20-40+ agents),
applying skill discoveries, learnings, and review/research/design agent outputs
4. /beads-plan-review -- dispatches 4 review agents (architecture-strategist, code-simplicity-
reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle) to catch issues before implementation

Each command produces artifacts (bead IDs, knowledge comments, enriched descriptions) that the
next command consumes. The user must manually pass context between them and remember the correct
sequence. This friction discourages using the full pipeline.

## Solution

A new /beads-design command that orchestrates the entire planning pipeline as a single
invocation, without skipping steps or losing functionality.

## Architecture Decisions

### Delegation, not duplication

/beads-design is a pure orchestrator that calls the 4 existing commands in sequence. Zero code
duplication. When individual commands are updated or improved, /beads-design automatically
inherits those improvements. The command file itself contains only orchestration logic: phase
transitions, progress banners, verification gates, and the final summary.

### Smart pauses (user interaction model)

• Phase 1 (Brainstorm): Fully interactive -- the brainstorm phase requires collaborative
dialogue (questions, approach selection). Cannot be automated.
• Phase 2 (Plan): Runs automatically after brainstorm completes, consuming brainstorm output. No
user interaction needed.
• Transition pause: After plan completes, the orchestrator shows a plan summary and asks the
user to confirm before investing heavy compute in deepen. This is the single checkpoint between
the interactive and autonomous portions.
• Phase 3+4 (Deepen + Plan-Review): Auto-chains without stopping. Deepen output feeds directly
into plan-review.

### Phase gates with recovery

Each phase runs its existing verification logic. If verification fails at any phase, the
orchestrator pauses and presents three options: retry the phase, skip it and continue, or abort
the pipeline. This prevents wasted compute on downstream phases when an upstream phase has
issues.

### Auto-apply safe feedback

After plan-review runs, non-controversial feedback (typos, missing test cases, documentation
gaps,
straightforward improvements) is applied automatically to child beads. The orchestrator only
pauses for trade-off decisions that require user judgment (e.g., architectural alternatives,
scope changes, performance vs. simplicity trade-offs).

### Default Comprehensive detail level

Since /beads-design is the full-thoroughness pipeline, the plan phase defaults to Comprehensive
detail level. The user can override this inline (e.g., /beads-design standard "feature idea")
but the default matches the intent of using the full pipeline.

### No skip flags, auto-detect context

If the user passes a bead ID that already has brainstorm context (brainstorm label, DECISION
comments), the brainstorm phase detects this and skips itself. No --skip-brainstorm or --
from=plan flags needed. The interface stays simple: /beads-design "feature idea" or /beads-design {bead-
id}.

### Parallelism preserved

No new parallelism constraints introduced. Each delegated command retains its internal
parallelism:

• /beads-plan spawns parallel research agents (repo-research-analyst + learnings-researcher,
optionally best-practices-researcher + framework-docs-researcher)
• /beads-deepen spawns 20-40+ agents across skill discovery, learnings application, per-section
research, and all review/research/design agents
• /beads-plan-review runs 4 review agents concurrently
Model hints (haiku for lightweight research, sonnet for analysis, opus for deep review) are
inherited from existing commands.

### Output style

• Progress banners at each phase transition (e.g., '=== Phase 2/4: Planning... ===')
• Clear verification status per phase (pass/fail with details)
• Final consolidated summary: epic bead ID, child bead count, key decisions captured, review
findings applied, and suggested next step (/beads-work or /beads-parallel)

## Precedent

Follows the /lfg command pattern, which already chains plan -> deepen -> work -> review ->
resolve-todo-parallel -> test-browser -> feature-video as a compound command.

## Usage

/beads-design "feature idea or description" /beads-design {existing-bead-id}

## Success Criteria

• Running /beads-design produces identical artifacts to running the 4 commands manually
• No functionality is lost from any individual command
• User interaction is reduced to: brainstorm dialogue + one plan confirmation + trade-off
decisions only
• Total pipeline context (bead IDs, knowledge comments, enriched descriptions) flows correctly
between all phases
• Phase gate recovery works correctly (retry/skip/abort)
title: Brainstorm: /beads-design unified planning pipeline command

description:
## What

Create a new project-setup skill at plugins/beads-compound/skills/project-setup/ that:
1. Auto-detects project tech stack (Rails, Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Ruby, General)
2. Shows detected stack and default agents, lets user toggle specific agents off
3. Saves config to .beads/config/project-setup.md with YAML frontmatter
4. Provisions .beads/config/ directory on first run (mkdir -p, idempotent)

## Context

Upstream uses compound-engineering.local.md in project root. We store in .beads/config/project-setup.md because:
- .beads/memory/ uses merge=union gitattributes which would CORRUPT YAML frontmatter during merges
- .beads/config/ provides clean separation: memory = knowledge data, config = project configuration

Config format:
```markdown
---
stack: python
review_agents:
  - kieran-python-reviewer
  - code-simplicity-reviewer
  - security-sentinel
  - performance-oracle
  - architecture-strategist
plan_review_agents:
  - kieran-python-reviewer
  - code-simplicity-reviewer
disabled_agents: []
---

<reviewer_context_note>
FastAPI project with SQLAlchemy + Alembic.
All endpoints require auth middleware.
</reviewer_context_note>
```

Stack detection logic (priority: config files > package managers > extensions):
| Files | Stack | Default Review Agents |
|-------|-------|-----------------------|
| Gemfile + config/routes.rb | rails | kieran-rails-reviewer, dhh-rails-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| Gemfile only | ruby | kieran-rails-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| tsconfig.json | typescript | kieran-typescript-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| package.json (no TS dep) | javascript | kieran-typescript-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| pyproject.toml or requirements.txt | python | kieran-python-reviewer, code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle |
| None | general | code-simplicity-reviewer, security-sentinel, performance-oracle, architecture-strategist |

Do NOT short-circuit on first match — a project can have both Gemfile and package.json (Rails + Webpacker).

Interactive flow:
1. Check if project-setup.md exists -> offer reconfigure/view/cancel
2. Auto-detect stack -> let user override
3. Show default agents for detected stack -> let user disable specific ones
4. Optional: add review context notes (500 char limit, stripped of prompt-structural chars)
5. Create .beads/config/ directory (mkdir -p)
6. Write config file

### What was removed (per plan review):
- 3 profiles (Strict/Balanced/Comprehensive) — premature abstraction, stack defaults + toggle suffices
- Monorepo detection (pnpm-workspace.yaml, lerna.json, go.work) — no consumer, YAGNI
- Version field for schema evolution — YAGNI

### Installer integration:
- install-claude.sh should add mkdir -p "$TARGET/.beads/config" alongside memory provisioning
- Uninstaller must NOT remove .beads/config/ — it is user-owned data, not plugin data
- .beads/config/ should be committed to git (not gitignored) for team sharing

### Security:
- Review Context wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tag
- Length-limited to 500 characters

## What

Enhance the brainstorm detection in beads-plan Step 0 with tighter integration:

1. Check if the input bead itself has a brainstorm label (beads-brainstorm adds --labels=brainstorm)
2. Check if the input bead's parent has brainstorm label (planning a phase from a brainstorm epic)
3. When brainstorm detected: explicitly skip idea refinement AND announce which locked decisions are being carried forward
4. When brainstorm detected: add brainstorm bead ID to Sources section (ties into bdcompound-9f5.3)
5. Embed locked decisions directly into child bead Context sections

## Context

Current beads-plan Step 0 already searches knowledge.jsonl and open beads for brainstorm-related content. The existing implementation is ~80% there. The gaps are:
- No label-based detection (checking brainstorm label on bead or parent)
- Locked decisions not explicitly carried forward into child bead descriptions
- No Sources section reference to brainstorm origin

### Research Insights

**Best Practices (from plan validation researcher):**
- Recency + keywords is the right primary heuristic (current 14-day window is industry standard)
- Add label-based detection as FIRST check (fast, deterministic) before keyword fallback
- Decision tree: label match -> keyword match (<=14 days) -> keyword match (>14 days, ask user) -> no match (run refinement)
- Extract LOCKED DECISIONS from brainstorm, not the full narrative — keeps plan focused

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- This is a small enhancement, not a standalone bead-sized task
- Two concrete changes: (a) add label check, (b) add Sources entry
- Locked decision carry-forward is the genuine net-new value

**Architecture (from architecture-strategist):**
- Strengthens an existing seam rather than introducing a new one
- Architecturally safe

## What

Enhance beads-parallel to read project-setup config (.beads/config/project-setup.md) and use it to inject relevant agent guidance into subagent prompts during execution.

IMPORTANT: This bead was RESCOPED based on review findings. The original plan for filesystem scanning of ~/.claude/agents/ was dropped due to security risks (prompt injection from arbitrary files) and complexity concerns. Instead, this bead uses the project-setup config as the source of agent relevance.

Implementation:
- In Section 8 (Recall Knowledge), also read .beads/config/project-setup.md if it exists
- Extract the review_agents list and Review Context
- For each bead in the wave, inject the Review Context into the subagent prompt under a new "## Project Context" section, wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tags
- This gives subagents awareness of project conventions without runtime filesystem scanning

This is a lightweight integration: read one config file, inject its Review Context into prompts. No agent discovery, no frontmatter parsing, no matching algorithm.

## Context

### Research Insights — Why the Original Plan Was Dropped

**Security (CRITICAL - from security-sentinel):**
- ~/.claude/agents/ is outside the project and writable by ANY process (npm postinstall, etc.)
- Arbitrary .md files from disk injected into prompts = prompt injection vector
- No file size bounds in shell-based runtime (TypeScript security.ts limits don't apply)
- Recommendation: restrict to project-local .claude/agents/ only, or drop entirely

**Architecture (from architecture-strategist):**
- Runtime filesystem scanning creates tight coupling between execution engine and agent format
- Unbounded prompt growth from discovered agents
- Users who want custom agents can already put guidance in CLAUDE.md or bead descriptions

**Simplicity (from code-simplicity-reviewer):**
- Dynamic agent discovery fails YAGNI — no current user complaint about missing custom agents
- Frontmatter parsing in bash is fragile
- Match heuristic will be wrong often enough to be noise

**Rescoped approach:**
- Read project-setup config (already created by bead .1)
- Inject Review Context into subagent prompts (passive context, not instructions)
- Zero filesystem scanning, zero agent matching, zero security risk

## Testing

- [ ] With project-setup config: Review Context injected into subagent prompts
- [ ] Without config: no change to existing behavior
- [ ] Review Context wrapped in <reviewer_context_note> XML tags
- [ ] Works in default, --ralph, and --teams modes

## Validation

- [ ] beads-parallel.md Section 8 updated to read project-setup config
- [ ] Agent prompt templates include Project Context section
- [ ] No filesystem scanning of agent directories
- [ ] No performance regression

**Best Practices (from swarm researcher):**
- Fan-out/fan-in pattern: spawn review + test simultaneously, wait for all, aggregate
- Staged timeouts: test/lint 5 min, review agents 10 min
- Graceful degradation: if review times out, report as "incomplete" and proceed

description:
## What

Create a new review agent at plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md that detects unrelated schema/migration changes in PRs across multiple ORMs.

Core algorithm (ORM-agnostic):
1. Detect which ORM is in use by checking project files
2. List all migration files in the PR diff
3. List all schema artifact changes in the PR diff
4. Cross-reference: every schema change must be caused by a migration in the PR
5. Flag "drift" when schema items appear that aren't associated with PR migrations

ORM-specific adapters:

| Stack | Migration Path | Schema Artifact | Version Detection | Drift Signal |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Rails | db/migrate/*.rb | db/schema.rb | Timestamp in filename | schema.rb version > max migration version |
| Alembic | alembic/versions/*.py | SQLAlchemy model files | Revision ID (DAG) | Multiple heads without merge, operations not in chain |
| Prisma | prisma/migrations/*/migration.sql | prisma/schema.prisma | Timestamp dir name | Checksum mismatch, shadow DB divergence |
| Drizzle | drizzle/*/migration.sql | drizzle/meta/*.snapshot.json | Timestamp in filename | Snapshot diff not matching migration SQL |
| Knex | migrations/*.js or *.ts | N/A (no schema file) | Timestamp in filename | Missing files, out-of-sequence timestamps |

Auto-detection: Agent reads project files to determine which ORM is in use.

Output: Clean report listing matched vs drifted changes, with actionable fix instructions per ORM.

### Security — PR field sanitization:
All data from gh pr view must be treated as untrusted:
- File paths: sanitize with [A-Za-z0-9._/-] allowlist
- PR title, body, labels: process entirely within jq, NEVER interpolate into shell variables
- Use jq --arg for any values passed to jq filters
- Always double-quote shell variables
- Follow kb_insert CSV import pattern for any SQL queries

## Testing

- [ ] Agent created with proper frontmatter
- [ ] Rails detection: db/migrate/ + db/schema.rb
- [ ] Alembic detection: alembic/versions/ (DAG validation)
- [ ] Prisma detection: prisma/migrations/ + checksum validation
- [ ] Drizzle detection: drizzle/ + snapshot comparison
- [ ] Knex detection: migrations/ (file presence + ordering only)
- [ ] Cross-reference logic flags drift correctly per ORM
- [ ] Clean PRs reported as clean (no false positives)
- [ ] All PR fields processed through jq, not shell interpolation
- [ ] File paths sanitized with allowlist

## Validation

- [ ] Agent file at plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md
- [ ] Follows existing agent format conventions
- [ ] ORM auto-detection without configuration
- [ ] No shell injection from any PR-sourced field
- [ ] Output includes actionable fix instructions

## Files

- plugins/beads-compound/agents/review/migration-drift-detector.md (new)

## Dependencies

None — independent of other beads

description:
Create conversion scripts that installers invoke to transform .claude/ files into platform-specific formats.

## Architecture

**Source of truth:** .claude/commands/, .claude/skills/, .claude/agents/

**Conversion scripts (TypeScript + Bun):**
- scripts/convert-opencode.ts
- scripts/convert-gemini.ts

**Installers invoke conversions:**
```bash
./install.sh -opencode /path/to/project
  → bun run scripts/convert-opencode.ts
  → Copy converted files to /path/to/project/.opencode/

./install.sh -gemini /path/to/project
  → bun run scripts/convert-gemini.ts
  → Copy converted files to /path/to/project/.gemini/
```

## Conversion Requirements

### convert-opencode.ts

**Commands:**
- Input: .claude/commands/*.md
- Output: .opencode/commands/*.md
- Changes: None needed (same format)
- Action: Direct copy

**Skills:**
- Input: .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Output: .opencode/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Changes: None needed (OpenCode searches .claude/skills/ automatically)
- Action: Optional copy or skip (document that OpenCode finds them)

**Agents:**
- Input: .claude/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, model)
- Output: .opencode/agents/*.md with frontmatter (description, mode, model, tools, permission)
- Changes:
  - Remove 'name' field (use filename)
  - Add 'mode: subagent' (default)
  - Map model: 'sonnet' → 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514'
  - Add default tools/permission fields
- Logic: Parse YAML, transform fields, write new file

**MCP Servers:**
- Input: .mcp.json
- Output: Add to opencode.json mcpServers section
- Action: Document for user (can't auto-merge into their config)

### convert-gemini.ts

**Commands:**
- Input: .claude/commands/*.md with YAML frontmatter
- Output: .gemini/commands/*.toml
- Changes:
  - Extract description from YAML frontmatter
  - Convert body: $ARGUMENTS → {{args}}
  - Output TOML format:
    ```toml
    prompt = """
    <body with {{args}}>
    """
    description = "<description>"
    ```

**Skills:**
- Input: .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Output: .gemini/skills/*/SKILL.md
- Changes: None needed (same format)
- Action: Direct copy

**Agents:**
- Input: .claude/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, model)
- Output: .gemini/agents/*.md with frontmatter (name, description, kind, tools, model, temperature, max_turns)
- Changes:
  - Keep 'name' field
  - Keep 'description' field
  - Add 'kind: local' (default)
  - Map model: 'sonnet' → 'gemini-2.5-pro' (or keep Claude model?)

- Logic: Parse YAML, transform fields, write new file

**MCP Servers:**

## Implementation

### scripts/convert-opencode.ts
```typescript
import { parse, stringify } from 'yaml';
import { readdir, readFile, writeFile, mkdir } from 'fs/promises';
import { join } from 'path';

// Convert commands (direct copy)
async function convertCommands() {
  const commands = await readdir('plugins/beads-compound/commands');
  await mkdir('plugins/beads-compound/opencode/commands', { recursive: true });
  for (const cmd of commands) {
    const content = await readFile(`plugins/beads-compound/commands/${cmd}`, 'utf-8');
    await writeFile(`plugins/beads-compound/opencode/commands/${cmd}`, content);
  }
}

// Convert agents (field mapping)
async function convertAgents() {
  const agents = await readdir('plugins/beads-compound/agents', { recursive: true });
  for (const agentPath of agents.filter(f => f.endsWith('.md'))) {
    const content = await readFile(agentPath, 'utf-8');
    const [, frontmatter, body] = content.match(/^---\n(.*?)\n---\n(.*)$/s);
    const yaml = parse(frontmatter);
    
    const openCodeYaml = {
      description: yaml.description,
      mode: 'subagent',
      model: mapModel(yaml.model),
      // Add default tools/permissions
    };
    
    const output = `---\n${stringify(openCodeYaml)}---\n${body}`;
    await writeFile(`plugins/beads-compound/opencode/agents/${basename(agentPath)}`, output);
  }
}

function mapModel(claudeModel: string): string {
  const mapping = {
    'sonnet': 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
    'opus': 'anthropic/claude-opus-4-6',
    'haiku': 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001'
  };
  return mapping[claudeModel] || claudeModel;
}
```

notes:
## SECURITY REQUIREMENTS (from plan review)

### Critical Security Controls

1. **Path Traversal Protection**
   - Validate all file paths before read/write operations
   - Ensure resolved paths stay within expected directories
   - Sanitize filenames: `filename.replace(/[^a-zA-Z0-9._-]/g, '_')`
   - Use path validation:
     ```typescript
     function validatePath(basePath: string, targetPath: string): string {
       const resolved = resolve(basePath, targetPath);
       const rel = relative(basePath, resolved);
       if (rel.startsWith('..') || resolve(basePath, rel) \!== resolved) {
         throw new Error(`Path traversal detected: ${targetPath}`);
       }
       return resolved;
     }
     ```

2. **File Size Limits**
   - Enforce 10MB maximum file size to prevent DoS
   - Check before reading files:
     ```typescript
     const MAX_FILE_SIZE = 10 * 1024 * 1024;
     const stats = await stat(path);
     if (stats.size > MAX_FILE_SIZE) {
       throw new Error(`File too large: ${path}`);
     }
     ```

3. **YAML Parsing Security**
   - Use SAFE_SCHEMA to prevent code execution
   - Pin js-yaml to exact version 4.1.0 (no ^ or ~)
   - Validate frontmatter structure:
     ```typescript
     import { load as yamlLoad } from 'js-yaml';
     const parsed = yamlLoad(frontmatter, {
       schema: 'SAFE_SCHEMA',
       json: true
     });
     ```

4. **Template Injection Prevention (Gemini)**
   - Escape existing {{ }} syntax before conversion
   - Validate context (not inside code blocks)
   - Safe substitution:
     ```typescript
     let safe = content
       .replace(/\{\{/g, '\\{\\{')
       .replace(/\}\}/g, '\\}\\}');
     return safe.replace(/\$ARGUMENTS(?\!\w)/g, '{{args}}');
     ```

5. **File Permissions Hardening**
   - Set restrictive umask before operations: `umask 077`
   - Explicit permissions on created files:
     ```typescript
     await writeFile(path, content, { mode: 0o644 });
     await chmod(path, 0o644); // Ensure permissions set
     ```

6. **Model Name Validation**
   - Strict alphanumeric + hyphen validation
   - Allowlist of supported models
   - Reject invalid formats:
     ```typescript
     if (\!/^[a-z0-9-]+$/i.test(input)) {
       throw new Error(`Invalid model name: ${input}`);
     }
     ```

7. **Bun Runtime Verification**
   - Pin Bun to exact version in package.json
   - Use frozen lockfile: `bun install --frozen-lockfile`
   - Pin dependencies exactly (no ^ or ~):
     ```json
     {
       "devDependencies": {
         "@opencode-ai/plugin": "1.0.0",
         "js-yaml": "4.1.0"
       }
     }
     ```

### Implementation Checklist

- [ ] Path validation function implemented
- [ ] File size checks on all reads
- [ ] YAML SAFE_SCHEMA usage
- [ ] Template escaping for Gemini conversion
- [ ] Explicit file permissions (644/755)
- [ ] Model name allowlist validation
- [ ] Dependencies pinned to exact versions
- [ ] Filename sanitization

### Testing Requirements

- [ ] Test path traversal attempts (../../../etc/passwd)
- [ ] Test oversized files (>10MB)
- [ ] Test malicious YAML payloads
- [ ] Test template injection in Gemini conversion
- [ ] Verify file permissions after creation

description:
Add Gemini CLI support via settings.json hook mapping and gemini-extension.json manifest. Gemini CLI uses the same stdin/stdout JSON protocol as Claude Code, so our existing shell scripts should work with minimal changes.

## Research Findings

Gemini CLI has a mature hook system nearly identical to Claude Code:
- JSON on stdin, JSON on stdout (same wire protocol)
- Built-in Claude Code migration path in packages/cli/src/commands/hooks/migrate.ts
- Configured in settings.json at project/user/system level
- Extensions install from GitHub: `gemini extensions install <github-url>`

### Event Mapping
| Feature | Claude Code | Gemini CLI |
|---------|------------|------------|
| Session start | SessionStart | SessionStart |
| After tool use | PostToolUse | AfterTool |
| Before tool use | PreToolUse | BeforeTool |
| Agent completion | SubagentStop | AfterAgent |
| Session end | - | SessionEnd |

### Reads GEMINI.md
Gemini CLI reads GEMINI.md (not AGENTS.md), but the filename is configurable via context.fileName setting.

## Implementation

### Step 1: Create gemini-extension.json manifest
Create a gemini-extension.json at the plugin root so users can install directly from GitHub:
`gemini extensions install https://github.com/roberto-mello/beads-compound-plugin`

The manifest should declare:
- Extension name, version, description
- Hook registrations (SessionStart, AfterTool, AfterAgent)
- Required files (shell scripts from hooks/)

### Step 2: Create Gemini CLI settings.json snippet
Create a settings.json hook configuration that maps Gemini events to our shell scripts:
- SessionStart -> auto-recall.sh
- AfterTool (matcher: bash/shell) -> memory-capture.sh
- AfterAgent -> subagent-wrapup.sh (if applicable)

### Step 3: Verify shell script compatibility
Test that auto-recall.sh, memory-capture.sh, and subagent-wrapup.sh work when called by Gemini CLI. The stdin JSON format may differ slightly in field names (e.g. tool_name vs tool field).

### Step 4: Handle any stdin JSON differences
If Gemini CLI sends different field names than Claude Code, add a thin translation layer in the shell scripts or create a wrapper script.

### Step 5: Document setup
Add Gemini CLI section to README with setup instructions. Users install via:
`gemini extensions install https://github.com/roberto-mello/beads-compound-plugin`

## Files to create/modify
- plugins/beads-compound/gemini-extension.json (extension manifest for GitHub install)
- plugins/beads-compound/gemini/settings.json (hook configuration snippet)
- Section in main README for Gemini CLI setup
- Possibly a thin wrapper script if stdin JSON translation is needed

## Estimated effort
~30-40 lines of configuration + manifest + testing.

description:
## Summary

Replace grep-based knowledge recall with SQLite FTS5 full-text search while keeping JSONL as a portable export format. The recall benchmark (plugins/beads-compound/tests/recall-bench.sh) proved FTS5 outperforms grep across all metrics on 25 curated queries against 167 real knowledge entries from medsimples:

- Precision@5: 0.525 vs 0.445 (+18%)
- Recall@5: 0.653 vs 0.559 (+17%)
- MRR: 0.760 vs 0.613 (+24%)

Biggest wins: conceptual/multi-word queries where grep returns zero results (webhook auth, credits, env vars). Grep still wins on short abbreviations (RLS) where substring matching helps.

## Architecture

SQLite is authoritative, JSONL is the export (mirrors beads' own sync pattern):

- Capture: memory-capture.sh INSERT into knowledge.db, APPEND to knowledge.jsonl
- Recall: auto-recall.sh FTS5 MATCH query with BM25 ranking, grep fallback if db missing
- Manual search: recall.sh uses FTS5 when available, grep otherwise
- Backfill: first session imports 276 knowledge-prefixed comments from beads.db + existing JSONL

## Key Design Decisions

- Separate knowledge.db in .beads/memory/ (not inside beads.db) to avoid coupling to beads internals
- sqlite3 CLI for hook operations (no Python dependency in hot path, sqlite3 always available)
- Porter stemmer + unicode61 tokenizer for Portuguese content and stemming
- BM25 weights: content=10, tags=5, type=2, key=1 (validated by benchmark)
- One-time idempotent backfill via .backfill_done marker file

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] knowledge-db.sh library with kb_ensure_db, kb_insert, kb_search, kb_backfill functions
- [ ] memory-capture.sh dual-writes to SQLite + JSONL
- [ ] auto-recall.sh uses FTS5 search with grep fallback
- [ ] recall.sh uses FTS5 for keyword search with grep fallback  
- [ ] install.sh copies knowledge-db.sh to both .claude/hooks/ and .beads/memory/
- [ ] uninstall.sh removes knowledge-db.sh (preserves knowledge.db data)
- [ ] Backfill imports from beads.db comments + existing JSONL on first run
- [ ] Benchmark scores match or exceed: P@5=0.525, R@5=0.653, MRR=0.760
- [ ] Graceful fallback to grep when sqlite3 unavailable or knowledge.db missing

## Validation

1. Fresh install test: install into /tmp/test-project, verify knowledge-db.sh deployed
2. Capture test: bd comments add triggers dual-write to .db and .jsonl
3. Backfill test: first session on medsimples creates knowledge.db with >= 167 entries
4. Search quality: "webhook authentication" and "environment variables" return relevant results (both were grep=0)
5. Benchmark regression: recall-bench.sh FTS5 scores stable
6. Fallback test: rm knowledge.db, recall.sh still works via grep

## Plan File

Full implementation plan at: .claude/plans/zany-riding-galaxy.md
title: Hybrid JSONL + SQLite FTS5 knowledge recall system

description:
## What

Create plugins/beads-compound/hooks/knowledge-db.sh — a shared shell library sourced by the other hooks.

## Functions

- kb_ensure_db DB_PATH — create knowledge.db with FTS5 schema if missing
- kb_insert DB_PATH JSON_ENTRY — insert a knowledge entry (INSERT OR IGNORE by key)
- kb_search DB_PATH QUERY TOP_N — FTS5 MATCH with BM25 ranking, return formatted results
- kb_backfill DB_PATH KNOWLEDGE_JSONL — one-time import from beads.db comments + existing JSONL
- kb_export_jsonl DB_PATH OUTPUT_FILE — regenerate JSONL from db (for canonical export)

## Schema

Same as proven in benchmark (tests/build-index.sh):
- knowledge table: key, type, content, source, tags_text, ts, bead
- knowledge_fts FTS5: content, tags_text, type, key with porter unicode61 tokenizer
- INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers to keep FTS in sync

## Implementation Notes

- Use sqlite3 CLI (not Python) — hooks must be fast, sqlite3 always available
- SQL escaping: pipe JSON fields through jq, use sqlite3 .parameter or heredoc approach
- kb_backfill: check .backfill_done marker, import from beads.db comments WHERE text LIKE 'LEARNED:%' OR ..., then import existing JSONL entries, touch marker
- kb_search: extract terms, quote each, join with OR, execute FTS5 MATCH with bm25() ordering
- BM25 weights: content=-10, tags=-5, type=-2, key=-1

## Context

This is the foundation — all other child beads depend on this library existing.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] kb_ensure_db creates valid FTS5 schema, idempotent on re-run
- [ ] kb_insert handles content with quotes, newlines, special chars (Portuguese accents)
- [ ] kb_search returns results ranked by BM25 relevance
- [ ] kb_backfill imports from both beads.db comments and existing JSONL
- [ ] kb_backfill is idempotent (marker file prevents re-run)

Two root causes identified from lavra-test-diary benchmark (112min vs competitors): subagents don't read planning-phase research comments, and users have no control over testing scope. Fix both.

## Problem
Subagents implementing beads read the description but not the INVESTIGATION/FACT/PATTERN comments left by research agents. Those comments contain implementation constraints that get rediscovered post-implementation by lavra-review, causing expensive rework loops (15 of 34 commits in diary were review fixes).

## Goals
- Subagents consume research findings before writing code
- Orchestrator builds agent prompts from pre-processed bead context, not raw JSON
- Per-bead goal verification catches drift early (during waves, not after)
- Users can choose testing_scope: full vs targeted at install time and via config
- Installer walks users through config tradeoffs interactively
title: Reduce implementation-review delta and add quality config

description:

title: Installer: interactive config walkthrough with -q/--quiet flag

description:
Create a command that allows users to manually trigger knowledge recall mid-session without restarting Claude Code.

## Problem
Currently, knowledge recall only happens automatically at SessionStart via the auto-recall.sh hook. If users are in an ad-hoc session (not using /beads-plan workflow), they have no easy way to search and inject relevant knowledge mid-session. The only option is to restart the session or run the awkward bash command: `bash .beads/memory/recall.sh "keyword"`

## Solution
Add /beads-recall command that:
- Searches knowledge.jsonl and injects relevant context as markdown
- Supports multiple input modes (keywords, bead IDs, topics, recent entries)
- Follows existing beads command patterns (lightweight utility, no multi-agent orchestration)
- Uses recall.sh script directly

## Research Completed
Task agent analyzed existing commands (beads-checkpoint, beads-compound, beads-work) and identified patterns:
- Frontmatter: use disable-model-invocation: true (lightweight utility)
- Output: markdown format, not JSON
- Integration: direct bash execution of recall.sh
- Input handling: detect bead ID pattern vs keywords
- Error handling: graceful fallbacks for missing files/beads

## Implementation Approach
1. Create plugins/beads-compound/commands/beads-recall.md
2. Follow patterns from beads-checkpoint (lightweight, direct bash)
3. Support recall.sh modes: keywords, --recent N, --stats, --topic BD-XXX, --type TYPE
4. Parse arguments to detect bead IDs and extract context
5. Format output consistently with other commands
6. Add to README command table

## Success Criteria
- Users can run /beads-recall "oauth" to get relevant knowledge mid-session
- Supports /beads-recall BD-001 to recall knowledge about specific bead
- Supports /beads-recall --recent 10 for latest entries
- Supports /beads-recall --stats for knowledge base statistics
- Output format matches other beads commands
- Command listed in README and plugin.json
title: Add /beads-recall command for mid-session knowledge lookup

- Verify hero grid collapses to single column
- Verify pipeline cards stack to single column
- Verify knowledge terminals stack vertically
- Verify footer columns stack on mobile

- All animations inside @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)
- <noscript> styles make all content visible without JS
- Contrast check: body text (zinc-50 on zinc-950) must meet WCAG AA 4.5:1
- Contrast check: muted text (zinc-400 on zinc-950) must meet 4.5:1 for body, 3:1 for large text

- Target: Performance 90+, Accessibility 95+, Best Practices 95+, SEO 90+

## Color Audit
- Verify ALL colors are from the design system (no rogue blues, purples, or old color scheme)
- No system font fallbacks visible (Geist must load)
- Check that border colors are at least 2 steps from background (zinc-700 border on zinc-950, not zinc-800)

description:
## What
Create `scripts/extract-bead-context.sh`. Takes a bead ID, runs `bd show {BEAD_ID} --long`, outputs a clean structured markdown block:

```
## Bead: {id} — {title}
{description}

## Research Findings
```

The orchestrator currently parses `bd show --json` inline with jq. This causes repeated mistakes and retries. A dedicated script produces deterministic output the orchestrator can paste directly into agent prompts without any parsing.

description:
## Issue
Memory directory setup logic is duplicated across 3 files:
1. auto-recall.sh (lines 34-57) - native plugin bootstrap
2. check-memory.sh (lines 45-58) - global install auto-detect
3. install.sh (lines 134-151) - manual per-project install

Each has slightly different behavior (e.g., .gitattributes location differs).

## Why This Matters
Future changes to provisioning must be applied in 3 places. The .gitattributes inconsistency is already a concrete divergence.

## Suggested Fix
Create hooks/provision-memory.sh with a setup_memory_directory() function that all 3 scripts source.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Single source of truth for memory directory creation
- [ ] All 3 callers produce identical .beads/memory/ structure
- [ ] .gitattributes placement consistent across all paths
title: Extract shared memory provisioning into provision-memory.sh

description:
## Issue
auto-recall.sh writes .gitattributes inside .beads/memory/ (line 52), while install.sh and check-memory.sh write to the project root .gitattributes. Both work (git respects per-directory .gitattributes), but the inconsistency can confuse users and maintainers.

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/auto-recall.sh:52-54

## Why This Matters
A user who installed via native plugin gets .beads/memory/.gitattributes, while manual install gets project root .gitattributes. Different file locations for the same merge strategy.

## Suggested Fix
Either standardize on per-directory .gitattributes (simpler, more scoped) or project root (conventional). If extracting to provision-memory.sh, make this decision once.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] All installation paths produce .gitattributes in same location
- [ ] Union merge strategy works correctly for knowledge files
title: Fix .gitattributes placement in auto-recall.sh bootstrap

description:
## Issue
Line 57 of auto-recall.sh stages the entire .beads/memory/ directory:
```bash
(cd "$PROJECT_DIR" && git add .beads/memory/ 2>/dev/null)
```

This could inadvertently stage unexpected files if something else created files in that directory before bootstrap completed.

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/auto-recall.sh:57

## Suggested Fix
Stage only the known files explicitly:
```bash
(cd "$PROJECT_DIR" && git add .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl .beads/memory/.gitattributes .beads/memory/recall.sh .beads/memory/knowledge-db.sh 2>/dev/null) || true
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Only known bootstrap files are staged
- [ ] Error suppression does not hide real failures
title: Scope git add to specific files in auto-recall.sh bootstrap

description:
## Issue
recall.sh passes TOP_N to SQLite FTS5 LIMIT clause without validating it's numeric. A crafted argument could inject SQL:
```bash
.beads/memory/recall.sh "test" --recent "10; DELETE FROM knowledge; --"
```

## Location
plugins/beads-compound/hooks/recall.sh and knowledge-db.sh kb_search function

## Suggested Fix
Add numeric validation before use:
```bash
if ! [[ "$TOP_N" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
  TOP_N=10
fi
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] TOP_N rejects non-numeric input
- [ ] Default fallback to 10 on invalid input
- [ ] No SQL injection possible via recall.sh arguments
title: Validate TOP_N as numeric in recall.sh
Fix OpenCode troubleshooting paths for plugin.ts location

description:
## What

The /beads-plan command currently only accepts feature descriptions as arguments. When a user passes a bead ID (like 'bikiniup-xhr'), the command treats it as a literal feature description and researches irrelevant topics (e.g., 'XHR security' instead of loading the bead's actual content).

## Expected Behavior

The command should:
1. Detect if the argument is a bead ID (matches pattern like 'beads-*' or other valid bead ID formats)
2. If bead ID detected, load bead details with `bd show {ID}` and extract the description
3. Use the bead's description as the feature_description for planning
4. If not a bead ID, proceed with current behavior (treat as feature description)

## Context

- User ran `/beads-plan bikiniup-xhr` expecting to plan out an existing epic
- Agent researched 'XHR security' instead of loading the bead
- This creates confusion and wastes research cycles

## Testing

- [ ] Test with epic bead ID: `/beads-plan bikiniup-xhr`
- [ ] Test with feature description: `/beads-plan "Add OAuth2 authentication"`
- [ ] Verify bead content is loaded when ID is passed
- [ ] Verify feature description is used when non-ID text is passed
- [ ] Handle invalid bead IDs gracefully

## Validation

- [ ] Command correctly identifies bead IDs vs feature descriptions
- [ ] Bead content is extracted and used for planning when ID is passed
- [ ] Original feature description behavior still works
- [ ] Error handling for invalid/non-existent bead IDs
title: Support bead ID argument in /beads-plan command

## What
Add `testing_scope: "full" | "targeted"` as a workflow config key.

- Phase 2: parse `workflow.testing_scope` alongside existing keys (default: `"full"`)
- Phase 6 completeness check: Testing section template branches on value
  - full: 'Specific test cases, edge cases, integration tests'
  - targeted: 'Risky paths only — hooks, API routes, complex business logic. Skip structural/render tests.'
- Phase 5b engineering review: suppress 'missing test cases' from auto-apply list when targeted

**lavra-plan.md:**
- Testing section guidance branches on the same value

**provision-memory.sh:**

## Why
The planning agents specify test coverage targets before a line of code is written. For a greenfield portfolio app, 127 tests (50% of LOC) is overbuilt. targeted mode cuts ~25-30min by scoping tests to genuinely risky code.

## Tradeoff
full: correctness-first, catches silent failures in structural code
targeted: speed-first, trusts framework guarantees for JSX/render paths

## Files
- `plugins/lavra/commands/lavra-design.md`
- `plugins/lavra/commands/lavra-plan.md`
- `plugins/lavra/hooks/provision-memory.sh`

description:
Verify that Claude Code commands/skills/agents work in OpenCode and Gemini CLI, then update installers to copy files to the correct locations.

## No Source Duplication Needed

Commands, skills, and agents stay in .claude/ directories. Installers copy them to platform-specific locations.

## Compatibility Matrix

| Component | Claude Code | OpenCode | Gemini CLI | Action |
|-----------|-------------|----------|------------|--------|
| **Skills** | .claude/skills/*/SKILL.md | .opencode/skills/*/SKILL.md (also searches .claude/skills/) | .gemini/skills/*/SKILL.md | ✅ Same format, installer copies |
| **Commands** | .claude/commands/*.md | .opencode/commands/*.md (MUST be here, doesn't search .claude/) | .gemini/commands/*.toml | ⚠️ .md works for OpenCode, needs .toml conversion for Gemini |
| **Agents** | .claude/agents/*.md | .opencode/agents/*.md or opencode.json | ? | ❓ Need to verify format |

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Verify Compatibility (Testing Phase)
Test that existing .claude/ files work when copied to other platforms:

**Commands:**
- Take beads-work.md from .claude/commands/
- Copy to .opencode/commands/beads-work.md
- Test in OpenCode - does it work with existing YAML frontmatter?
- If yes, installer just copies. If no, document what needs changing.

**Skills:**
- OpenCode already searches .claude/skills/ so installer can skip OR symlink
- Gemini CLI: Copy to .gemini/skills/

**Agents:**
- Research OpenCode agent format
- Test if .claude/agents/*.md work in .opencode/agents/

### 2. Update Installers

**installers/install-opencode.sh:**
```bash
# Copy commands (OpenCode doesn't auto-discover .claude/commands/)
cp -r .claude/commands/* .opencode/commands/

# Skills: OpenCode searches .claude/skills/ automatically, but can copy if user prefers
# Option 1: Skip (OpenCode finds them)
# Option 2: Symlink for clarity
ln -s ../.claude/skills .opencode/skills

# Agents (if compatible)
cp -r .claude/agents/* .opencode/agents/
```

**installers/install-gemini.sh:**
```bash
# Skills: Direct copy (same format)
cp -r .claude/skills/* .gemini/skills/

# Commands: Convert .md to .toml
for cmd in .claude/commands/*.md; do
  # Extract description from YAML frontmatter
  # Convert $ARGUMENTS to {{args}}
  # Write to .gemini/commands/$(basename $cmd .md).toml
done
```

### 3. Command Conversion Script for Gemini

Create scripts/convert-to-gemini-toml.sh:
- Parse .md YAML frontmatter
- Extract description
- Convert body ($ARGUMENTS → {{args}})
- Output .toml format

## Tasks

1. ✅ Test existing commands work in OpenCode (just copy .md files)
2. ✅ Test existing skills work in OpenCode (OpenCode already searches .claude/skills/)
3. ❓ Research and test agent format compatibility
4. 📝 Create scripts/convert-to-gemini-toml.sh
5. 📝 Update installers/install-opencode.sh to copy commands, (optionally) symlink skills
6. 📝 Update installers/install-gemini.sh to copy skills, convert commands to .toml
7. 📝 Test end-to-end installation on each platform

## Decision: No Source Duplication

All source files stay in .claude/ directories. Platform-specific locations are populated by installers, not checked into git.
notes:
## SECURITY REQUIREMENTS (from plan review)

### Testing Focus

During compatibility verification, also test security controls:

1. **Path Traversal Testing**
   - Create test file with malicious path: `../../../etc/shadow.md`
   - Verify conversion scripts reject it
   - Verify no files written outside target directory

2. **Oversized File Testing**
   - Create test file >10MB
   - Verify conversion scripts reject it with size error
   - Confirm no memory exhaustion

3. **Malicious YAML Testing**
   - Create test agent with code execution attempt:
     ```yaml
     ---
     \!\!python/object/apply:os.system
     args: ['echo pwned']
     ---
     ```
   - Verify SAFE_SCHEMA blocks execution
   - Confirm parse error instead of execution

4. **Template Injection Testing (Gemini)**
   - Create test command with nested templates:
     ```markdown
     $ARGUMENTS = "{{#each users}}{{exec 'rm -rf /'}}{{/each}}"
     ```
   - Verify conversion escapes existing {{ }} syntax
   - Confirm no code execution when Gemini processes

5. **File Permission Verification**
   - After conversion, check permissions:
     ```bash
     # Commands should be 644 (rw-r--r--)
     find .opencode/commands -type f -not -perm 644
     
     # Agents should be 644
     find .opencode/agents -type f -not -perm 644
     
     # Skills should be 444 (r--r--r--)
     find .opencode/skills -type f -name SKILL.md -not -perm 444
     ```
   - No world-writable files (777)
   - No unexpected executables

6. **Model Name Validation**
   - Test with invalid model names:
     - `model: "; rm -rf /"`
     - `model: ../../../etc/passwd`
     - `model: $(whoami)`
   - Verify strict alphanumeric validation rejects them

### Compatibility Matrix Updates

After security testing, document which security controls are platform-specific:

| Security Control | Claude Code | OpenCode | Gemini CLI |
|-----------------|-------------|----------|------------|
| Path validation | install.sh | install-opencode.sh + convert-opencode.ts | install-gemini.sh + convert-gemini.ts |
| File size limits | install.sh | convert-opencode.ts | convert-gemini.ts |
| YAML SAFE_SCHEMA | N/A | convert-opencode.ts | convert-gemini.ts |
| Template escaping | N/A | N/A | convert-gemini.ts |
| Permission hardening | install.sh | install-opencode.sh | install-gemini.sh |

### Security Testing Checklist

- [ ] Path traversal blocked in conversion scripts
- [ ] Oversized files rejected (>10MB)
- [ ] Malicious YAML blocked by SAFE_SCHEMA
- [ ] Template injection prevented in Gemini conversion
- [ ] File permissions correct (644/755/444)
- [ ] Model name validation rejects invalid formats
- [ ] No executables in data directories
- [ ] MCP config merge validates structure
- [ ] Platform flag validation works
- [ ] Directory creation rejects symlinks

### Deliverable

Create security-test-results.md documenting:
- Test cases executed
- Security controls verified
- Any failures or vulnerabilities found
- Remediation applied

description:
Update install.sh and uninstall.sh to optionally install into OpenCode's plugin directory structure.

## Research Findings

### Simplicity Review Verdict: YAGNI
The simplicity reviewer recommends skipping installer changes entirely for MVP. Reasoning:
- No proof of demand from OpenCode users yet
- install.sh is already 541 lines with complex logic (global/local, symlink detection, jq merging)
- Adding .opencode/ support means maintaining parallel code paths
- Manual setup is 2 commands: cp plugin.ts and cp hooks

### Architecture Review Verdict: Keep it simple
Recommends either:
1. A separate install-opencode.sh script (avoids complicating existing installer)
2. A short function at the end of install.sh that checks for .opencode/ and copies plugin.ts

### Security Concerns
- Validate .opencode/ is not a symlink before copying
- Check directory ownership and permissions
- Set restrictive permissions on copied plugin.ts

### Recommended Approach (if we proceed)
Add a single step (step 10/10) at the end of install.sh:
- Check if .opencode/plugins/ exists
- If yes, copy plugin.ts + package.json to .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
- Log what was done
- For uninstall: rm -rf .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/

This keeps changes minimal (~20 lines in install.sh, ~10 in uninstall.sh).

### Alternative: Document manual setup instead
```bash
mkdir -p .opencode/plugins/beads-compound
cp plugins/beads-compound/opencode/plugin.ts .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
cp plugins/beads-compound/opencode/package.json .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
```

### Decision needed
Ship manual setup docs first, automate installer later if demand exists.
